The Scholiarch's Residence.description: An elegant mansion of white marble overlooking the Grand Forum, where the High Scholiarch receives ambassadors, wealthy patrons, and the city's most distinguished minds. The central atrium features a mechanical orrery crafted by House Pontius engineers, showing the paths of celestial bodies with uncanny precision. A private garden grows rare medicinal plants used in theurgical practice.

The Temple Precinct.description: A sanctuary of stone columns and vaulted archways dedicated to the Sun King and the divine order of the furies. Pilgrims make daily offerings of grain and incense. Priests in flame-red robes perform morning rituals, their voices echoing through the stone. Below the main temple lies a sealed stairway—the entrance to the lowest library, forbidden for generations.

Scholion.name: Scholion
Scholion.basicInfo: A sprawling academy built into the stone bowl of an ancient caldera, its temples and dormitories cascading down the inner slopes like a white marble waterfall. Master teachers maintain schools of thought in separate compounds; students progress upward through levels of understanding, literally and figuratively. The air hums with incantation-practice; young sorcerers test their fury-bindings on carefully prepared targets, and their voices rise in coordinated chants at dawn. Water channels run down every street, cool and pure, fed by mountain springs. The rhythm is almost monastic, yet vibrant with the energy of learning.
Scholion.hiddenInfo: Scholion was built atop the ruins of an elder-empire military academy—the original structure is still mostly intact beneath the current compound. In the deepest levels, experiments in fury-binding continue in secret, conducted by a rogue order of scholars who believe the imperial way is stagnant. They are close to a breakthrough that could amplify a single warrior's furycraft a hundredfold—or drive them to madness.

The Central Amphitheater.description: A tiered stone arena where public disputations and demonstrations of furycraft occur daily. Master teachers stand in the center, summoning small flames, moving water, or bending metal as illustration for their lessons. Students pack the benches, arguing among themselves about the validity of techniques. The air is often thick with test-fire—small controlled flames that singe and spark.

The Dormitory Terraces.description: A series of stepped compounds where students live in spartan chambers, two to a room. The older students occupy higher terraces, closer to the ridge. Common kitchens serve communal meals. Laundry lines stretch between buildings, and the sound of young voices practicing languages and incantations fills the air from dawn to dusk. Fights occasionally break out between students of rival schools.

The Practice Yards.description: Vast open grounds where students test their fury-bindings under instructor supervision. Targets stand in graded rows—first-years use clay pots; advanced students work with carved stone. The ground is scorched and cratered from thousands of exercises. Safety wardens walk the perimeter, ready to quell any binding that goes awry. The smell of hot stone and burnt earth hangs perpetually.

The Master's Halls.description: Elegant stone buildings where senior teachers maintain private libraries and laboratories. Each master's hall is tailored to their discipline—a thermurgist's hall is perpetually warm and smells of sulfur; a fluionist's space is cool and echoes with rushing-water sounds. These halls are restricted to advanced students; the apprenticeship system here is rigid and competitive.

The Silent Archive.description: An underground library carved from living rock, its walls inscribed with the names of every master teacher who has ever worked at Scholion. Scrolls and tablets line the shelves in meticulous order. The chamber itself is acoustically treated—even a whisper becomes a roar if you're not careful. This enforces a rule of absolute silence, broken only by the soft scratch of students taking notes.

The Shattered Acropolis.name: The Shattered Acropolis
The Shattered Acropolis.basicInfo: The ruined heart of an elder-empire city, now a vast archaeological dig where scholars and antiquarians claw through millennia of stone and dust. Massive columns lie toppled across plazas; intact temples stand like ghosts amid rubble. Teams of workers with iron tools chip away at encrusted earth, searching for intact artifacts and readable inscriptions. The air is thick with stone-dust and the constant scrape of tools. Tents cluster among the ruins where teams of specialists document findings. Occasionally, diggers unearth something extraordinary—a sealed chamber, a cache of pre-imperial coins, or a statue so perfect it seems to defy the passage of ages.
The Shattered Acropolis.hiddenInfo: Three years ago, diggers discovered a sealed subterranean temple beneath the main acropolis. Inside, they found evidence that the elder-empire had achieved a form of furycraft far more sophisticated than current practice—perhaps even more powerful. The imperial scholars have suppressed the discovery, stationing guards and restricting access. But rumors have leaked. Something waits in that sealed chamber, and powerful interests are racing to claim it first.

The Main Excavation.description: The central plaza of the ancient acropolis, now a maze of trenches and scaffolding where teams of diggers work methodically through layers of soil and rubble. Wooden frames support potentially unstable masonry. Carts of dirt are hauled away constantly; screens stand in rows, used to sift through soil for small artifacts. The lead archaeologist, a stern woman named Lucia, oversees the work with exacting precision. Arguments frequently erupt between rival teams over access to promising sites.

The Temple Complex.description: A cluster of three temples arranged around a central courtyard, their pillars still mostly standing but fractured and weathered. One temple retains most of its roof, creating a shadowed chamber where scholars study inscriptions by torchlight. The stonework here is exquisite—carved reliefs depict figures in poses that suggest fury-binding practices. Pigment traces suggest the original carvings were painted in vivid colors, now faded to ghost-impressions.

The Sealed Vault Entrance.description: A massive bronze door, ancient beyond measure, set into the mountainside at the acropolis's eastern edge. It is sealed with both physical locks and inscribed wards of impressive complexity. Imperial guards stand permanent watch, turning away curious scholars. Behind the door lies a stone stairway descending into darkness. The sound of slow, methodical work can sometimes be heard from within—deliberately quiet, as if someone does not wish to disturb something sleeping below.

The Antiquary's Camp.description: A sprawling collection of tents and temporary shelters where scholars and specialists catalog findings. Long tables display pottery shards, coins, tools, and fragments of statuary. The air is thick with debate—experts argue about dating, provenance, and interpretation. A private tent houses the most significant finds, under heavy lock. Young apprentices spend hours carefully cleaning artifacts with soft brushes and chemical solutions. The smell of preservation oils and incense mingles with dust and canvas.

Mercaton.name: Mercaton
Mercaton.basicInfo: A sprawling market city where merchants from across the world converge, set at the intersection of three grand roads maintained by House Pontius. The city is pure commerce—caravans load and unload constantly; traders haggle in a dozen languages; banks exchange currencies under fortified stone roofs. At the city's heart stands the Great Market House, a colonnaded structure of stunning proportions where the wealthiest merchants rent permanent stalls. The pace is frenetic but organized; House Pontius engineers have created an efficient system of warehouses, roads, and water channels that keeps goods flowing like blood through veins. The smell of spices, leather, and animal dung mingles with the voices of thousands.
Mercaton.hiddenInfo: Mercaton's true power lies not in trade but in intelligence. House Pontius operates a vast spy network from the merchant houses here, using caravan routes to move information across the empire as readily as goods. The Pontian factor who coordinates this network works from a sealed chamber beneath the Great Market House, and her network has foiled three assassination attempts on the emperor in the past decade. She knows more secrets than any living person.

The Pilgrimage Road.name: The Pilgrimage Road
The Pilgrimage Road.basicInfo: A famous trading and pilgrimage route stretching across the Eastlands, punctuated by way-stations, shrines, and rest-posts maintained by both merchant guilds and religious orders. The road itself is beautifully paved with fitted stone, a marvel of House Pontius engineering. Merchant caravans travel it in organized convoys for safety; pilgrims walk it on foot, often in groups, singing as they go. The landscape unfolds along the route—farmland giving way to grassland, then rocky approaches to distant temples. At night, way-stations glow with lamplight and cook-fires; the road itself becomes a constellation of travelers.
The Pilgrimage Road.hiddenInfo: The Pilgrimage Road crosses the territory of bandits who have sophisticated knowledge of caravan schedules and routes. These are no common brigands—they coordinate with someone in House Pontius, intercepting specific shipments and allowing others to pass unmolested. The arrangement is so seamless that no one outside the conspiracy suspects it. A particular merchant is actually a Pontian agent, using the bandits to redirect goods to secret locations across the Eastlands.

The Library of Forgotten Names.name: The Library of Forgotten Names
The Library of Forgotten Names.basicInfo: Built into a hillside overlooking a valley of fruit orchards, this library specializes in pre-imperial records and elder-language texts. The main hall is a vault of pale stone; reading-chambers branch off like cells, each carefully climate-controlled by House Pontius water-channels and clever stone work. The collection is smaller and more specialized than Heliasium's library, but far more exotic—texts on elder-empire history, astronomical records from civilizations dead a thousand years, fragmentary grammars of lost languages. The librarians are scholar-monks who have taken vows of silence and live within the library's walls. They move like ghosts between the stacks, gesturing to direct visitors to their desired manuscripts.
The Library of Forgotten Names.hiddenInfo: The head librarian, Sister Caelix, has discovered fragments of texts describing a civilization that existed before the elder-empire—one that possessed mastery over the furies that makes current practice look like children's games. She has been secretly translating these texts, and has identified the location of what may be the last intact artifact of that earlier age. She is torn between her vow of silence and the knowledge that this discovery could remake the world. Only one other person knows—a scholar from Scholion who arrives in secret each season.

The Temple of Echoes.name: The Temple of Echoes
The Temple of Echoes.basicInfo: Perched on a hilltop overlooking a vast valley, this monastery-temple is dedicated to meditation and contemplative study of the furies' nature. The main temple is a perfect acoustic chamber where chanted prayers reverberate beautifully; monks spend hours in synchronized chanting, their voices creating harmonics that seem to resonate with the fury-winds themselves. The complex includes meditation cells, study gardens with rare plants used in theurgical practice, and a healing hall where the infirm come seeking the monks' ministrations. The approach is a long spiral path lined with stone markers, each inscribed with a passage of philosophical wisdom. Pilgrims arrive year-round seeking guidance and spiritual healing.
The Temple of Echoes.hiddenInfo: The monks have discovered that their synchronized chanting actually amplifies the subtle fury-winds in the valley, creating a measurable effect on the physical world around them. The temple was originally built by imperial architects who understood this principle. The current Abbot believes this knowledge represents a path to furycraft that doesn't require the direct binding of individual warriors—a communal, harmonious approach that could eliminate the need for elite fury-wielders entirely. Such knowledge could destabilize the empire's military hierarchy.

Pontius Station Seven.name: Pontius Station Seven
Pontius Station Seven.basicInfo: A fortified way-station built where a great aqueduct feeds water into a vast cistern, creating a natural oasis surrounded by functional architecture. The station is a perfect square of stone, with barracks, storage facilities, and administrative buildings arranged around a central courtyard where merchants and travelers gather. House Pontius engineers maintain the aqueduct system here with meticulous care—water flows through the station according to precise timetables. The station commander, a veteran officer, runs the facility with military efficiency. Caravans stop here to water their animals and take on supplies; soldiers are stationed here year-round to protect the vital infrastructure.
Pontius Station Seven.hiddenInfo: The aqueduct feeding this station has two channels—one public, one hidden beneath the first. The secret channel delivers water to underground cisterns that feed forward supplies to a military fortress in the mountains beyond, unknown to the emperor. House Pontius is preparing for something, stockpiling resources in secret. The station commander is one of only five people who know the truth, and she is beginning to have doubts about the conspiracy she's part of.

The Ruins of Aeternum.name: The Ruins of Aeternum
The Ruins of Aeternum.basicInfo: A sprawling complex of ancient stone structures in a high mountain valley, frozen in time by catastrophe. The buildings show evidence of extreme heat—some stones are blackened as if by an inferno, others are fused together in places. The city layout suggests incredible sophistication; the central plaza once held a fountain larger than any currently known to exist. Walls bear inscriptions in elder-language, many still legible though their meaning escapes current scholars. The site is remote but famous among antiquarians—those who can reach it often find something extraordinary. The air here feels heavy with old magic, and the stones themselves seem to retain heat even in winter.
The Ruins of Aeternum.hiddenInfo: Aeternum was destroyed in a single moment of catastrophic magical release—a fury-binding experiment or battle that went catastrophically wrong. The damage patterns suggest that the fury-winds themselves were weaponized in a way that current theorists believe should be impossible. Sealed beneath the central plaza is a chamber containing the remains of whoever or whatever was at the center of that release. The remains have partially calcified into something neither entirely stone nor entirely biological. If brought to Heliasium, the revelation could cause a fundamental reevaluation of furycraft's limits.

The Antiquary's Rest.name: The Antiquary's Rest
The Antiquary's Rest.basicInfo: A modest but well-appointed inn built on a hilltop overlooking the valley where The Shattered Acropolis sits. The inn is famous throughout the scholarly world as the gathering place for antiquarians, archaeologists, and historians chasing fragments of the past. The proprietor, an elderly scholar himself, maintains an exceptional library in the common room—texts on excavation techniques, elder-language guides, maps of lost cities. Travelers here swap discoveries, compare theories, and form expeditions. The dining hall echoes with debate; corners of the common room are staked out by rival scholars who maintain hostile but collegial relationships. The inn's wine is excellent, and the beds are comfortable—a rarity in this remote region.
The Antiquary's Rest.hiddenInfo: The proprietor, a scholar named Marcus, is actually an imperial agent gathering intelligence on the most ambitious antiquarians and their findings. He reports regularly to House Pontius, carefully steering conversations to learn what scholars believe they've discovered about pre-imperial civilizations. He's conflicted about his role—he genuinely loves the pursuit of knowledge and resents being forced to betray the trust of his guests. He's been secretly helping promising antiquarians find resources the imperial bureaucracy would deny them.

Portus Aurorae.name: Portus Aurorae
Portus Aurorae.basicInfo: The jewel of the eastern coast now chokes under siege. Golden-stone walls bristle with Aquilan war-engineers' catapults and fury-fueled pyres; behind them, the famed harbor sprawls choked with scuttled merchant ships used as breakwaters. The air reeks of saltpetre, burning olive groves, and human desperation. Soldiers in Aquilan copper clash with Tharsi infiltrators in the winding market-streets. The wealthy cling to the heights above the harbor, while refugees swamp the docks in search of passage, their coin and jewelry traded for spots in fishing boats headed for anywhere-else. The boom of siege-engines shakes the cobblestones at dawn.
Portus Aurorae.hiddenInfo: The harbor master is Tharsi-turned-Aquilan—he knows every smuggler's inlet and feeds names to both sides for gold. Beneath the main temple vault lies a cache of Tharsi blood-idols, seized decades ago but never consecrated; Tharsi mages know precisely where they are and will slaughter thousands to recover them. The richest families have already fled with their treasure; what remains is middle-class panic and lower-deck desperation. A secret tunnel runs from the palace wine-cellars to an old water-gate on the south cliff—the garrison commander uses it to slip contraband past the Tharsi siege-engines.

The Harbor Waterfront.description: A chaos of merchant stalls, fishing nets, and refugees queuing for passage. Aquilan soldiers extort fleeing families; Tharsi agents pose as traders, hunting stragglers. The smell of salt, tar, and fear mingles with the cries of children separated from parents.

The Market Quarter.description: Winding stone streets lined with shuttered shops and makeshift shelters. War-profiteers hawk weapons, food at inflated prices, and forged passage papers. Every corner holds a dealer in black-market supplies—furs for siege-refugees, healer's salves, laudanum for the shell-shocked.

The Garrison.description: A sprawl of barracks, armories, and war-hospitals buzzing with Aquilan soldiers. Wounded men lie screaming in infirmaries run by over-worked field surgeons who barter with death daily. The parade ground reeks of sweat and leather. Desertions are executed publicly.

Upper Fortifications.description: Massive stone ramparts crowned with Aquilan fury-casters and stone-throwers. From here, the besieging Tharsi forces are visible across the blackened fields. The walls hum with wood-craft and blood-magic collision—where Tharsi blood-curses meet Aquilan furycraft, the very stone weeps. Supply carts arrive at night; casualties are removed the same way.

The Docks.description: A forest of scuttled ship-hulls and improvised docks where fishing vessels and smuggler's boats take on refugees for exorbitant sums. Smugglers work openly here, knowing the Aquilan command turns a blind eye (for a cut). Tharsi spies watch from rooftops. Every boat departure is a lottery of who drowns and who escapes.

The Refugee Quarter.description: A sprawling slum of canvas tents, makeshift hovels, and packed tenements in the shadow of the eastern wall. Starvation, disease, and despair breed violence. Black-market surgeons work from tents. Tharsi agents infiltrate here, recruiting desperate souls as spies or saboteurs. The garrison barely maintains order.

The Siege-Camp of Tharsis.name: The Siege-Camp of Tharsis
The Siege-Camp of Tharsis.basicInfo: A sprawling canvas city of blood-mages, slave-soldiers, and draconic war-priests encamps in the blackened fields west of Portus Aurorae. Vast siege-engines of bound bone and living wood—grown from Tharsi blood-craft—loom over the camp. The ground here is fouled with ritual circles, dried blood, and the bones of sacrificed horses. Tharsi shamans chant in the darkness, while slave-wranglers crack whips over conscripted troops. Smoke from a thousand cook-fires rises toward a sky permanently darkened by blood-curse miasma. The air tastes of copper and ash.
The Siege-Camp of Tharsis.hiddenInfo: The Tharsi siege will break within two lunar cycles—their magic is burning through bloodlines faster than they can feed it. Three major blood-priests have already died to the curse, taking knowledge of Aquilan countermeasures with them. A Tharsi warlord secretly negotiates with the House Pontius commander for early surrender terms; if discovered, it will ignite civil war within the Tharsi army. The war-elephants penned here are starving; they will soon be sacrificed for blood-fuel. An entire regiment of Tharsi soldiers—conscripted from a conquered province—plans mutiny; they wait only for Aquilan forces to press the siege-lines.

The Blood-Ritual Circle.description: A vast ring of carved stone and ritual marks at the camp's heart. Here, Tharsi priests perform the daily sacrifices that fuel their siege-magic. The ground steams with spilled vitality. Slaves are brought here at dawn and sunset; screams echo across the encampment.

The War-Engine Pavilion.description: Towering siege-engines of bone, sinew, and living wood stand here like sleeping giants. Each costs a hundred lives in blood-sacrifice to operate. Engineers sharpen stone teeth; handlers whip teams of enslaved men into position. The smell of rot and raw magic is suffocating.

The Command Pavilion.description: A sprawling tent-fortress where Tharsi warlords plot strategy and blood-priests scry for weakness in Aquilan defenses. Maps marked with blood cover the war-table. Messengers come and go constantly. Guards here are elite and brutal; no outsiders are tolerated.

The Slave-Pens.description: Vast corrals of misery—thousands of enslaved and conscripted soldiers await orders or sacrifice. Disease runs rampant. Tharsi overseers patrol with whips and spears. An undercurrent of mutiny simmers just below the surface of compliance.

The Siege-Lines.description: Endless ranks of soldiers, mages, and war-beasts arranged in siege formations facing Portus Aurorae. Here, Tharsi blood-mages clash daily with Aquilan furycraft across no-man's-land. The ground is a cemetery of the recently dead. Reinforcements arrive constantly; departures are far rarer.

The Crumbling Bastion of Castel Marino.name: The Crumbling Bastion of Castel Marino
The Crumbling Bastion of Castel Marino.basicInfo: Once a proud fortification crowning the coastal heights, Castel Marino is now a skeletal monument to attrition. Its eastern tower leans drunkenly, half-sheared away by Tharsi siege-stones; the walls bristle with hastily-planted Aquilan fury-anchors that hum with defensive magic. The courtyard is crammed with supply wagons, wounded soldiers, and panicked clerks managing the war effort. Stone dust and ash perpetually cloud the air. Here, the forward command operates—this is where decisions are made that send hundreds to their deaths. The garrison is thin, spread across rotting fortifications that defy the laws of architecture by still standing.
The Crumbling Bastion of Castel Marino.hiddenInfo: The fortress's structural collapse is only weeks away—the war-engineers know it but say nothing, fearing panic. The commander has already prepared a secret withdrawal route through old smuggler tunnels beneath the eastern wall. A Tharsi infiltrator has been inside the fortress for three months, posing as a wounded soldier; she has memorized the entire layout and is waiting for the signal to burn the supply warehouses. The well was poisoned two weeks ago; the garrison has been drinking from a sealed reservoir no one speaks of, and it is running dry.

The War-Room.description: The nerve center of the forward command. Maps covered in blood-stains mark the siege-lines. Generals and captains bark orders; messengers arrive gasping with reports. Coffee is perpetually brewing. No one sleeps here.

The Courtyard.description: A pandemonium of activity—supply wagons being unloaded, wounded soldiers being carried to the infirmary, engineers inspecting the crumbling walls. The ancient cobblestones are slick with spilled blood and water from buckets used to quench fires.

The Infirmary.description: A converted barracks where field surgeons work without sleep, sawing limbs, cauterizing wounds, and dispensing laudanum to the dying. The stench is indescribable. Screams are constant. Infection is a death sentence; most infected die here.

The Armory.description: A low stone hall packed with weapons, armor, and ammunition. Constantly depleted, constantly restocked. The air is thick with metal-oil and grindstone dust. Blacksmiths work here, reforging shattered swords and reinforcing armor.

The Officer's Quarters.description: Austere chambers where the commander and his inner circle rest when they can. Maps of escape routes are hidden in the commander's private chambers. A single brazier provides inadequate warmth in the stone cold.

The Drowning Refugee Quarter.name: The Drowning Refugee Quarter
The Drowning Refugee Quarter.basicInfo: A sprawling maze of canvas tents, makeshift wooden structures, and packed humanity pressed into the lowlands east of Portus Aurorae. Twenty thousand souls—displaced farmers, merchants without goods, soldiers' families, the desperate and hopeless—wait here for evacuation that may never come. Disease spreads through the crowded lanes. Black-market dealers hawk rotted food and dangerous medicines. Children beg. The elderly die quietly in the dark. Tharsi spies circulate freely, recruiting the most desperate for sabotage. At dawn, the smell of human waste and sickness hangs thick enough to choke on.
The Drowning Refugee Quarter.hiddenInfo: A epidemic of bloody flux is gestating here—if it spreads into Portus Aurorae, the port will fall within a fortnight. The quarter's unofficial leader, a priestess of a banned earth-god, is secretly organizing an uprising; she plans to either force evacuation through riot or create a corridor for Tharsi forces as leverage. A Tharsi plague-mage has been deliberately spreading infection, hoping to trigger exactly this collapse. Three thousand refugees have already died; the actual death toll is hidden from Aquilan command.

The Field-Surgeon's Pavilion.name: The Field-Surgeon's Pavilion
The Field-Surgeon's Pavilion.basicInfo: A sprawling canvas tent-city nestled in the ravines south of the siege-lines, where Aquilan field-surgeons and healers perform miracles and monstrosities in equal measure. Men arrive here screaming with limbs shattered by siege-stones; they depart in pine boxes or on crutches, forever changed. The air is thick with the smell of blood, copper, and laudanum. Surgeons work without sleep, their hands perpetually stained red. Apprentices carry away buckets of severed flesh. This is a place where hope and despair wage constant war.
The Field-Surgeon's Pavilion.hiddenInfo: The head surgeon, Master Caelius, has discovered a compound derived from Tharsi blood-plants that accelerates healing—he acquired it through forbidden trade with a Tharsi herbalist. Its use is technically treason, but he uses it anyway, saving lives the official methods cannot. Three surgeons here are actually Tharsi infiltrators, documenting Aquilan casualties and unit strength for the enemy. A wounded soldier lying in the back corner is a Tharsi spymaster; no one knows yet.

The Contested Harbor-Mole.name: The Contested Harbor-Mole
The Contested Harbor-Mole.basicInfo: A long breakwater of ancient stone jutting into the bay, studded with crumbling watchtowers and hastily-erected fortifications. This narrows the harbor entrance; Aquilan forces hold it, but Tharsi war-galleys press constantly for control. The mole is a blood-soaked killing ground where soldiers fight hand-to-hand over a few feet of stone. Catapult-stones splash into the bay here daily. Bodies are thrown overboard to make room for the next wave of reinforcements. The water beneath is dark with blood.
The Contested Harbor-Mole.hiddenInfo: The mole is slowly sinking—mortar and stone have been eroded by decades of salt and neglect. Aquilan engineers know it will collapse completely within three lunar cycles. Tharsi scouts have discovered this; they are simply waiting for the fortress to destroy itself rather than waste soldiers. Beneath the mole, in submerged chambers built by ancient architects, lies a cache of sealed clay vessels—some filled with pre-siege gold bullion, others with documents proving the mole's entire foundation is unstable. A Tharsi diver has already retrieved one vessel; more expeditions are coming.

The War-Shrine of Bellona.name: The War-Shrine of Bellona
The War-Shrine of Bellona.basicInfo: A modest stone temple dedicated to Bellona, goddess of just conflict, stands on a bluff overlooking no-man's-land. Soldiers come here to pray before their final assaults, to carve their names into stones, to leave tokens for lovers they know they will not see again. The temple smells of blood—both ceremonial libations and the genuine article, tracked in on soldiers' boots. The priestess here, an ancient woman named Aelia, grants no absolution, only acknowledgment. Candles burn perpetually for the dead.
The War-Shrine of Bellona.hiddenInfo: Priestess Aelia is a former Oracle—she still hears the goddess's whispers, but they have turned dark. Bellona speaks to her of needless deaths, of Aquilan command wasting lives for pride. Aelia secretly funnels information to a Tharsi priestess of their blood-goddess, hoping that coordinated faith might end the war faster. Both women are committing what their respective faiths would call heresy. The temple's true name and purpose are hidden—it is actually a shrine to the old earth-gods, the ones worshipped before Rome came, and Bellona's altar conceals far older offerings.

The Smuggler's Cove of Abyssal Rocks.name: The Smuggler's Cove of Abyssal Rocks
The Smuggler's Cove of Abyssal Rocks.basicInfo: A treacherous inlet hidden among jagged rocks and cave-mouths on the southern coast, where smugglers run blockades nightly, bringing in food, weapons, and medicine for Portus Aurorae. The rocks are coated with phosphorescent algae that glows at night, making navigation deadly; only pilots who know the currents survive. Boats arrive under darkness; by dawn, their cargo is dispersed through secret channels into the city. Tharsi patrols search for this place constantly but have not yet found it—it is known by reputation only, a place that may not exist.
The Smuggler's Cove of Abyssal Rocks.hiddenInfo: The cove is controlled by a smuggler-lord named Castor, who plays both sides—he brings supplies to Portus Aurorae and sells intelligence to Tharsi contacts for twice the price. The Aquilan garrison commander knows this and tolerates it because the smuggling keeps the city from starving. A secret archive hidden in the cave-system contains manifests of every boat that has passed through in the past decade—if discovered, it would expose dozens of Aquilan officers involved in black-market trading. Castor has already prepared to scuttle the archive and vanish if the Tharsi find him first.

The Broken Lighthouse of Cape Despair.name: The Broken Lighthouse of Cape Despair
The Broken Lighthouse of Cape Despair.basicInfo: A skeletal structure of blackened stone jutting from a desolate cape, its beacon long extinguished. Tharsi siege-stones shattered it weeks ago; now it stands as a monument to Aquilan losses. Refugees sometimes shelter in its ruined base, but exposure and falling rubble make it deadly. Scavengers climb its unstable stairs looking for copper fixtures to sell. At night, Tharsi ships use it as a navigation marker, turning what should be an Aquilan beacon into a tool for the enemy.
The Broken Lighthouse of Cape Despair.hiddenInfo: The lighthouse keeper, an old man named Theodric, never fled—he is still in the ruin, blind and half-mad, still trying to light the beacon every night with oil salvaged from corpses of seabirds. He is living on rainwater and nothing else. Tharsi ships have begun to deliberately avoid the lighthouse's location, leaving him confused—he does not know his nightly ritual has become futile. Hidden beneath the lighthouse foundation, in a sealed chamber, is a mechanism left by ancient engineers—if activated, it could trigger a collapse that would kill anyone within a mile radius, including Theodric himself.

The No-Man's-Land of Burnt Groves.name: The No-Man's-Land of Burnt Groves
The No-Man's-Land of Burnt Groves.basicInfo: A devastated strip of blackened earth between Aquilan and Tharsi lines, where nothing grows and nothing survives. The olive groves that once fed the region have been burnt to ash by siege-fire. The ground is cratered by catapult strikes and salted with blood-curse residue from Tharsi magic. Skeletal trees stand like accusing fingers. No official armies hold this ground; instead, it is the realm of scavengers, deserters, infiltrators, and spies. At dawn, the ghosts of the dead seem to walk here.
The No-Man's-Land of Burnt Groves.hiddenInfo: A hidden encampment of Aquilan deserters survives here, led by a captain who rejected the war. They trade with both sides—information, supplies, safe passage for refugees willing to betray their commanders. At least one Tharsi war-band has made an uneasy pact with them; they do not attack each other, an arrangement that would be execution for treason if discovered. A plague of restless spirits inhabits the burnt groves—not Tharsi magic, but the genuine dead, unable to rest. They are most active at the hour before dawn.

Portus Ventidii.name: Portus Ventidii
Portus Ventidii.basicInfo: The great naval stronghold of House Ventidius sprawls across a fortified harbor ringed by limestone cliffs. War-galleys stand shoulder to shoulder at merchant quays; the air reeks of pitch, tar, and the salt-crusted sweat of ten thousand dockhands. Siege towers guard the approaches, and the constant thunder of the forge rings from ship-wright docks where hulls are rebuilt faster than the enemy can sink them. Every stone pulses with the industry of war—no leisure here, only discipline, ambition, and the relentless press of oars upon the waves.
Portus Ventidii.hiddenInfo: Beneath the war-harbor lies a vast network of sea-caves where House Ventidius hides stolen Murena vessels and hoards of captured furystone. The dockmaster, Centurion Falco, accepts bribes in furycraft knowledge—he has secretly traded naval intelligence to independent merchants for seven years, though his loyalty remains unswerving to the House.

Warship Yards.description: Massive timber frames rise like skeletal giants, surrounded by the shriek of saws and the ring of caulking mallets. Master shipwrights bark orders to crews patching hulls still scarred from battle. The air tastes of fresh-hewn oak and blood-salt from the treatment ponds.

Merchant Quays.description: Controlled chaos—merchants argue with customs officers while longshoremen haul cargo amid coiled rope and stacked amphoras. The quays are watched constantly by military overseers. Smugglers know to trade in taverns, not here.

Arsenal District.description: A fortress within the fortress. Weaponry gleams in orderly racks: boarding pikes, grappling hooks, ballistae ammunition. The smell of oil and metal mingles with acrid smoke from the forges. No civilian enters without escort.

The Salt Inns.description: A crooked sprawl of taverns and boarding houses where sailors blow coin on wine and the company of fishwives. Fights erupt without warning; the town guard tolerates only bloodshed that doesn't interfere with the docks.

Civitas Murena.name: Civitas Murena
Civitas Murena.basicInfo: The jeweled port of House Murena glitters with marble colonnades and merchant prosperity. Sleek merchant vessels crowd the harbors alongside fast-galleys painted in House colors. The city sprawls across terraced hills overlooking sheltered coves, where wool dyes run the water purple and the scent of cinnamon spice mingles with brine. Slave-gangs haul cargo from across the known world; senators in fine linen oversee trade that feeds the war-machine. Beauty masks ruthlessness here.
Civitas Murena.hiddenInfo: The House's real wealth flows from an undiscovered furycraft monopoly in the deep dye-vats—a rare mineral harvested only here produces the most potent tailings for metalcraft. The dye-masters guard the secret with hired assassins. Additionally, a network of spies operates from the merchant-houses, covertly funneling intelligence to Ventidian agents hidden among the slaves.

The Merchant Quarter.description: Columned warehouses of pale stone, their doors sealed with wax-seals bearing the House sigil. Ledger-men in fine tunics move between offices, tallying wealth that would make emperors weep. The air thrums with quiet, calculating greed.

Senate District.description: Marble temples and the seat of House governance overlook the harbor from manicured gardens. Only the powerful walk these streets freely; common folk glimpse them from afar. Politics deadlier than any naval battle unfolds behind closed doors.

The Dye-Vats.description: A sprawling complex of stone cisterns belching fragrant steam, where wool and cloth take on shades unknown elsewhere in the world. Enslaved labor toils in the chemical haze; accidents are common and unlamented. The air burns the eyes and reeks of strange salts.

Harbor's Edge.description: Where the ordered city ends and the docks begin—a liminal space of warehouses and rope-walks. Faster-paced than the merchant quarter, rougher than the senate district. Information and illicit goods trade hands here more openly than in the city proper.

The Wraith.name: The Wraith
The Wraith.basicInfo: A notorious pirate haven disguised as a fishing settlement on a low, rocky island. Ramshackle buildings cling to the slopes; fishing boats with patched sails crowd a natural harbor that connects to hidden caves and lagoons. The smell—woodsmoke, salt, and the stench of unwashed bodies—carries for miles. Every face here has committed murder; every hand shows scars. The Wraith's captain, a scarred woman called Erygia, maintains brutal order through wit and ruthlessness. Three great houses have tried to burn it. None succeeded.
The Wraith.hiddenInfo: Erygia's Wraith serves as a shadow broker for both Houses—she sells stolen furycraft secrets to the highest bidder and maintains a network of informants in every major port. Beneath the settlement lies a collapsed temple to a pre-imperial sea-god; within its halls, Erygia hides a archive of maps and fugitive scrolls that would make emperors tremble. Her crew numbers nearly five hundred, but only twelve know where the treasure truly lies.

The Driftwood Quarter.description: A ramshackle labyrinth of fishing shacks lashed together with ship-rope. Nets dry everywhere; the ground is carpeted with scales and fish-guts. Locals pretend to fish; the pickings are actually far below. This is where identity dies and reinvention begins.

Erygia's Hall.description: A fortress disguised as a merchant-house, its doors reinforced with stolen ship's timber. Inside, a throne of lashed bone overlooks tables where pirate captains negotiate raids and divide plunder. The walls are covered in maps and wanted notices. Few leave this room unchanged.

The Armory.description: A converted sea-cave lined with weapons taken from a hundred defeated vessels—cutlasses, boarding-pikes, ballista bolts, and rare furycraft-enhanced steel glinting in torchlight. This is where power is physically kept, and it is never left unguarded.

The Hidden Coves.description: A network of natural stone harbors hidden behind narrow stone teeth, accessible only at high tide or to those who know the secret channels. Here, stolen vessels are renamed, refitted, and sold. No charts exist—the way is remembered, not recorded.

The Lighthouse of Marius.name: The Lighthouse of Marius
The Lighthouse of Marius.basicInfo: A soaring column of pale stone crowned with a perpetual furycraft-fire rises from a barren, wind-scoured isle. The lighthouse has guided—and deceived—merchants for three centuries. Its keeper, an ancient man called Marius, tends the flame with ritualistic precision, though few know whether he serves the Houses or himself. Waves crash ceaselessly against the rocks below. Gulls wheel in endless spirals, their cries mixing with the moan of the wind. The only structure besides the tower is a small shrine whose god is long forgotten.
The Lighthouse of Marius.hiddenInfo: Marius is a latent furycrafter—his fire does not require oil, drawn instead from a bloodletting pact made sixty years past. He has slowly been poisoned by the pact; his death will extinguish the light permanently, unless his successor also agrees to the blood-price. He has written his successor's name in the shrine's hidden chamber, but sealed the door with a riddling glyph. The true master of the light is an ancient water-spirit that demands quarterly offerings—Marius leaves them at the tide line under cover of darkness.

Salapia's Nets.name: Salapia's Nets
Salapia's Nets.basicInfo: A sprawling fishing village where dozens of wooden houses cluster around a crescent beach. Nets hang everywhere—drying, being mended, being cast. The smell of smoked fish overwhelms all other odors. Women in sun-darkened skin work alongside children and old men, their hands perpetually raw from salt and line. Despite the naval war raging miles away, Salapia tries to remain neutral, selling fish to any port that pays. The village's matriarch, an equally weathered woman named Salapia herself, rules through consent rather than force.
Salapia's Nets.hiddenInfo: Salapia hides a devastating secret—her youngest grandson was pressed into a House Murena crew and died in a naval engagement. She has privately agreed to aid Ventidian agents with intelligence on Murena movements in exchange for a guarantee of safety for her village. However, Murena's sea-captains have grown suspicious; an assassination attempt last month missed her by inches.

The Teeth of Praxos.name: The Teeth of Praxos
The Teeth of Praxos.basicInfo: A jagged reef system where black rocks jut from churning white water like broken teeth. The reef has claimed uncounted ships. Even experienced pilots fear its approach—the currents are vicious, the rocks hidden and hungry. Yet the reefs shelter something precious: the deepest waters here create a natural barrier, and in calm moments, merchant vessels can anchor in protected pools invisible from open sea. Sailors say the reef moans with the voices of drowned men. The sound may be wind through stone, or it may be something far more purposeful.
The Teeth of Praxos.hiddenInfo: The reef is the tomb of an ancient war-galley—a super-ship built by an empire long dead, whose furycraft weapons remain partially functional deep beneath the waterline. Salvage divers occasionally retrieve fragments of its hull-steel, stronger than anything currently forged. Something vast moves in the deepest waters here; sailors report glimpses of impossible shapes during storms. It may be myth, or it may be guardian and tomb-keeper both.

The Whispering Shoals.name: The Whispering Shoals
The Whispering Shoals.basicInfo: Sandbars and rocky shallows stretch across miles of the inner sea, creating a labyrinth only local pilots can navigate. The bottom is thick with ancient wreckage—not just ships, but stone foundations of buildings older than the Houses themselves. At certain times of year, when the light hits the shallow water just so, shapes of impossible architecture become visible beneath the sand. Smugglers favor this region; patrols from either House struggle with the treacherous navigation. The water here is cold and tastes of ancient stone.
The Whispering Shoals.hiddenInfo: The Shoals are the ruins of the pre-imperial civilization that originally settled the Mare Internum. Fragments of advanced furycraft survive in the deepest channels—tools and weapons that predate the Houses by centuries. A secret society called the Archivists has been salvaging these artifacts for generations, building a hidden library within the safest wreck. The Shoals whisper because water moves through countless cavities in the ruins, creating harmonic tones—or perhaps something far more intentional.

The Smuggler's Atoll.name: The Smuggler's Atoll
The Smuggler's Atoll.basicInfo: A ring of palm-covered islands surrounding a lagoon of such brilliant turquoise it seems unreal. The atoll appears deserted to casual observers—just a handful of beached hulks and weathered structures. Yet it is the primary trading post for merchants unwilling to enter either House's official ports. Neutral ground maintained by an alliance of independent merchants, the Atoll operates on honor-debt and shared interest. The clear water is deceptive; beneath lies a graveyard of sunken cargo and the bones of those who violated the Atoll's laws.
The Smuggler's Atoll.hiddenInfo: The Atoll is secretly controlled by representatives of a third power—an exiled merchant-family from the northern coast with ambitions to establish their own House through wealth and leverage. They are gradually accumulating furycraft weapons and knowledge, positioning themselves as a future military force. The tropical lagoon conceals an undersea cave system where they've hidden a vault of stolen treasures and forbidden scrolls. At least one major House has discovered this secret and is planning an incursion.

The Temple of the Drowned.name: The Temple of the Drowned
The Temple of the Drowned.basicInfo: An island crowned with limestone columns, half of them ruined, forming what may once have been a temple or may be the weathered remnants of something far older. The structure sits at the edge of the Mare Internum's deepest waters. Pilgrims and the desperate come here to cast offerings into the sea—gold, weapons, furystone, even written confessions weighted with stones. The shrine-keepers (there are always three, though their identities change) interpret the tides and currents as divine will. Bodies occasionally wash ashore with expressions of profound peace on their drowned faces.
The Temple of the Drowned.hiddenInfo: The Temple is not merely a shrine—it is an interface with something far beneath the sea. The current keeper, a scarred priestess named Neera, can communicate with it, though she claims not to understand what speaks back. The temple accepts offerings because something consumes them, leaving traces of heat in the water. Naval disasters occur with uncanny timing near the Temple; it may prevent them, or it may cause them. The truth remains hidden.

The Blockade Chain.name: The Blockade Chain
The Blockade Chain.basicInfo: A series of fortified stations strung across the narrowest waters of the Mare Internum, anchored on rocky islands and maintained by whichever House holds temporary naval supremacy. Currently a Ventidian position, the Blockade consists of three principal forts connected by patrol galleys and rope-bridge systems that can be raised or lowered. Soldiers man the walls day and night, watching for breach-attempts. The waters below are fouled with wreckage and corpses from failed runs—a graveyard that warns all who approach.
The Blockade Chain.hiddenInfo: The Blockade's commander, Tribune Castor, has been secretly negotiating with House Murena for nearly a year, planning to reverse the blockade in a coordinated surprise attack. He has been methodically poisoning the water-supplies of his fellow officers to ensure their distraction during the changeover. Additionally, beneath the primary fort, smugglers have maintained a tunnel system for years—the Tribune knows of it and uses it to transport correspondence. At least three separate intelligence networks operate simultaneously within the Blockade's walls.

Karthaxis, City of the Crimson Spires.name: Karthaxis, City of the Crimson Spires
Karthaxis, City of the Crimson Spires.basicInfo: The pulsing heart of the Tharsi sea-empire sprawls across a volcanic peninsula, its obsidian-tiled avenues teeming with merchants, slaves, and robed priests. Black-galley harbors churn with activity, their hulls stacked with silks, spices, and strangest of all—vials of blood sealed with wax. Towers of red stone pierce the smoky sky, crowned with brass sigils of Malachor and Liloth. The air reeks of incense, salt, and something burnt and sweet that never fully disperses. Pleasure-palaces rise alongside slave-markets; opulence drenches every corner, yet beneath the jeweled surfaces flows a current of dread—the city's wealth is written in chains and blood-debts.
Karthaxis, City of the Crimson Spires.hiddenInfo: Karthaxis was built atop ruins of a much older civilization—the foundations contain sealed crypts where elder entities slumber. The Crimson Council (high blood-priests) meets in the Sunken Sanctum beneath the city, where they feed sacrificial victims directly into chambers said to open onto the domains of Malachor and Liloth. The slave-markets hide a thriving underground network of escaped slaves who communicate through coded spice-merchant signals. Three merchant-princes compete for dominance, and their proxy-wars burn whole districts; the current ruling family maintains power through a pact with a Siren-thing dwelling in the harbor depths.

Crimson Harbor.description: The great port where black-galleys load and unload cargo under the watchful eyes of priest-inspectors. The docks writhe with activity: slave-gangs hauling spice-crates, merchants haggling in a dozen tongues, and bloodstained priests performing benedictions over new vessels. The water itself seems darker here, fouled by decades of sacrificial washings.

Merchant's Quarter.description: A labyrinth of fortified warehouses, counting-houses, and the palatial residences of merchant-princes. Silk banners flutter between buildings; the air carries competing perfumes—cinnamon, myrrh, and the strange copper-sweet reek of blood-wine being aged in cellars. Assassination is an accepted cost of doing business here.

Slave Bazaar.description: An open plaza where thousands mill each day—some buying, most being bought. Wooden platforms raise the merchandise: foreign warriors, craftspeople, healers, and unfortunates torn from conquered lands. The noise is deafening; chains rattle, auctioneers shriek prices, and the enslaved weep or stare with hollow eyes. Here, human dignity is merely another commodity with a price-tag.

Temple of the Bleeding Eye.description: A colossal structure of obsidian and red stone, its entrance marked by a fifty-foot carved eye weeping blood. Inside, columns pulse with crimson light from alchemical flames. The innermost sanctum—forbidden to common folk—is where the high priests perform the rites that feed the devouring gods. The temple's lower levels descend into darkness; priests speak of them only in terrified whispers.

Palace of the Golden Chain.description: The current ruler's residence, a sprawling mansion of marble and gold overlooking the harbor. Guards in ritual armor stand at every gate. Within, pleasure-courtesans attend to the elite, and rooms overflow with treasures looted from a hundred conquered ports. The palace treasury holds not just gold and gems, but vials of virgin blood, each labeled with the conquered nation it came from.

The Obsidian Pyre-Grounds.name: The Obsidian Pyre-Grounds
The Obsidian Pyre-Grounds.basicInfo: Beyond the city's eastern walls sprawls a vast ceremonial complex of blackened stone and ash. Dozens of towering pyres stand arranged in precise geometric patterns, their charred surfaces stained rust-dark from centuries of burning. The air writhes with heat-shimmer even when no flames burn; priests claim the ground itself remembers the blood soaked into it. At the complex's heart looms the Grand Pyre—a structure thirty feet tall where the most sacred (or most condemned) sacrifices ignite. On feast-days for Malachor and Liloth, thousands gather to witness the flames, their chanting rising until it drowns out the screams.
The Obsidian Pyre-Grounds.hiddenInfo: The pyre-grounds were constructed over a rift where the veil between worlds grows thin. During sacrifices, shadows sometimes move independently of their casters, and the smoke forms shapes that seem to descend toward the earth rather than rise toward sky. Priests whisper that the devouring gods actually stand beneath the pyres, that sacrifice feeds them more directly than anywhere else. A mad scholar claims the ashes themselves are alive—cursed remnants of the sacrificed that still scream inaudibly. Several pyre-keepers have gone insane, claiming they hear the victims calling from the ashes, begging to be burned again.

Outer Processional.description: The broad avenue where processions arrive, lined with pylons carved with the sigils of Malachor and Liloth. Vendor-stalls selling sacred oils, ceremonial wines, and written prayers line the route. The smell of incense mingles with blood-stench from aged pyres.

Circle of Pillars.description: Thirty ancient pillars arranged in concentric circles, each carved with names of the sacrificed—or if their identities were lost, with the date of their burning. Scholars come here to research bloodlines, to find if an ancestor's name is carved in stone. Some pillars weep something dark in the pre-dawn darkness.

Grand Pyre Platform.description: The ceremonial center: a vast stepped platform crowned with the Grand Pyre itself. Stone benches rise in tiers around it for priests and dignitaries. Below the platform, a chamber carved into living rock holds the victims before their burning. The air above the pyre shimmers constantly, and those who breathe deeply report hallucinations of drowning in blood.

The Sunken Temple of Liloth.name: The Sunken Temple of Liloth
The Sunken Temple of Liloth.basicInfo: A partially submerged temple complex carved into a coastal cove, its white-stone columns half-drowned in salt water. The water glows faintly at dusk—bioluminescent algae, priests claim, though some whisper it is the eyes of something vast dwelling in the depths. The temple's visible portions gleam with precious stones; the submerged sections disappear into waters so dark that light seems to die in them. During high tide, the inner sanctum floods entirely; priests perform secret rituals in the underwater chambers, emerging hours later gasping and speaking in languages they don't remember learning. The temperature here is wrong—always cold, even when the sun beats mercilessly elsewhere.
The Sunken Temple of Liloth.hiddenInfo: Liloth is not merely a devouring god—she is an older thing, something that existed before gods had names. The temple was built on the site where she first spoke to mortal priests a thousand years ago. The submerged sanctum is where her voice is strongest; those who go deep enough can hear it, a sound like a woman weeping backwards through time. The priests perform a ritual called 'the Drowning Communion' where selected initiates are held underwater until they stop struggling—most die. Those who survive return changed, speaking in riddles, their eyes reflective like a creature's. Bodies of failed sacrifices wash ashore for miles in every direction; fishermen refuse to work this coast.

Upper Galleries.description: The dry portions of the temple above the high-tide line. Vast columned halls display treasures offered to Liloth: jewelry, weapons, goblets of foreign gold. Altar-tables are stained dark despite constant cleaning. Priests in white robes move silently here, performing maintenance on the temple.

Ritual Platform.description: An open-air plaza where public ceremonies take place during day-hours when the inner sanctum is inaccessible. Here, the chosen are anointed and blessed before descending to the underwater chambers. The platform is ringed with drainage channels that run dark water, and the sound of it dripping echoes upward from depths unseen.

The Drowning Stairs.description: A spiral staircase carved into the temple's heart, descending from dry stone into seawater. Each step goes deeper into cold. At the bottom—unreachable during low tide—lies the Sanctum Submerged, where Liloth's voice echoes from walls that have never known sunlight. Ropes with marks indicate depth-progression; priests speak of descending past the point where human ears should cease to function, yet hearing only becomes clearer as you go down.

Port Malachus: The Slaver's Haven.name: Port Malachus: The Slaver's Haven
Port Malachus: The Slaver's Haven.basicInfo: A sprawling harbor fortress nestled in a natural bay, its docks dedicated entirely to the slave trade. Ship after ship disgorges human cargo: prisoners of war, debt-slaves, the condemned, and victims of crimping and theft. The port reeks of human misery—unwashed bodies, fear-sweat, and the iron-tang of blood. Fortified slave-pens radiate outward from the docks, each capable of holding thousands. Auction rings operate day and night, dealing in fresh meat for the quarries, fields, and galleys of Tharsis. The fortress itself is a showcase of cruelty: watchtowers bristle with weapons, whipping-posts stand in public plazas, and chains decorate the buildings like jewelry.
Port Malachus: The Slaver's Haven.hiddenInfo: Port Malachus is named for an ancient slaver-saint whose bones rest in a crypt beneath the governor's hall. Once yearly, the governor performs a ritual where he bathes in the blood of a child slave—this is said to grant him visions of profitable trade routes and warning of rebellions. The slave population outnumbers the free by fifty to one; escape attempts are brutally suppressed, and the corpses are left hanging for weeks. Rumor speaks of an underground railroad operated by sympathetic merchants and broken priests, hiding refugees in secret chambers beneath the pens. The governor's personal guard are all war-widows sworn to vengeance; they serve only because their families are held hostage in Karthaxis.

The Spice-Plague Bazaar.name: The Spice-Plague Bazaar
The Spice-Plague Bazaar.basicInfo: In the shadow of a volcanic ridge lies the great marketplace where merchant-lords trade in Tharsis's most coveted wares: rare spices worth their weight in gold, exotic poisons, alchemical reagents, and stranger things whispered about in shadows. Hundreds of stalls stretch in organized rows, their canopies a riot of color. The air hangs thick with competing aromas: cardamom, saffron, brimstone, and something faintly rotten that might be dried corpse-flowers. Merchants haggle fiercely; fortunes change hands in breath-moments. The bazaar operates under strict guild law, enforced by masked peacekeepers whose allegiance to profit supersedes all morality. A merchant here can sell what they wish—as long as the house-takes its percentage.
The Spice-Plague Bazaar.hiddenInfo: Many of the 'spices' are actually ground bone from sacred sacrifices or ash from the pyre-grounds, each carrying curses or blessings depending on the buyer's faith. The most exclusive section, sealed behind a blood-marked door, trades in poisons so potent that a grain can kill an elephant—and substances that reverse death, at least temporarily. The bazaar operates on a sophisticated network of spies; every transaction is reported to the guild-masters, who use the information to broker power. Several merchants are actually priests in disguise, noting which buyers purchase poison and which purchase blessing, for later... attention.

Palace of Prince Vesperian.name: Palace of Prince Vesperian
Palace of Prince Vesperian.basicInfo: Perched on a promontory overlooking the black-galley harbors stands the sprawling palace of one of Tharsis's most powerful merchant-princes. Marble cloisters surround courtyards where fountains run dark with wine, and statuary depicts both divine and profane scenes in voluptuous detail. The palace's hundreds of chambers house a small army of servants, concubines, and guards. The treasury vaults—said to be guarded by something far worse than soldiers—hold treasures that could ransom kingdoms. Within, Prince Vesperian holds court, dispensing judgment on rival merchants, sealing treaties with blood-oaths, and hosting lavish feasts where the entertainment sometimes involves slaves fighting to the death for sport.
Palace of Prince Vesperian.hiddenInfo: Prince Vesperian is not truly human—he is something far older, maintained in this form through ritual sacrifices that his priests perform monthly. His true nature is a secret guarded by his inner circle; outsiders are only permitted to see him by lamplight, never by sun. His palace treasury contains not just gold, but magical artifacts from empires that fell millennia ago. The prince maintains a secret breeding program, attempting to create a race of perfect slaves through careful selection and... something else, something forbidden. His favorite concubine is a spirit in mortal form, bound to him through a pact made centuries before his current incarnation.

The Bloodwood Shipyards.name: The Bloodwood Shipyards
The Bloodwood Shipyards.basicInfo: A sprawling industrial complex where the legendary black-galleys of Tharsis take shape from rare timber and dark magic. Hundreds of workers—slaves and free-laborers alike—hammer, saw, and weave the ships together under the constant watch of taskmasters. The wood itself seems to drink light, its surface absorbing the sun's rays and returning nothing. The air tastes of blood and resin. Construction teams work in brutal shifts; injury and death are routine. When a ship is completed, priests perform a christening ceremony that involves bloodletting and the binding of spirits into the vessel's wood—sailors claim the ships remember their launch-days and sometimes weep at dusk.
The Bloodwood Shipyards.hiddenInfo: The bloodwood comes from trees that grow only in one cursed forest, where the soil is saturated with the blood of ancient atrocities. The trees themselves are sentient in a way; craftsmen who work the wood too long report hearing whispers and feeling watched. The lead shipwright is a war-captive who designed vessels for Tharsis's enemies before being enslaved; he has slowly been poisoning the construction, building fatal flaws into every ship—his sabotage has already sent three black-galleys to the ocean bottom. The yard manager has discovered the treason but has made a deal with the shipwright: if the sabotage stops, the warship will be spared, and the manager will help him escape. The secret burns between them.

The Scholar's Archive of Forbidden Tongu.name: The Scholar's Archive of Forbidden Tongues
The Scholar's Archive of Forbidden Tongu.basicInfo: Hidden within a hillside overlooking the city stands a library of astonishing proportions, its stone facade carved with a thousand languages—some recognizable, most not. Here, priests and merchants both seek knowledge: battle-strategies, historical records, and most coveted, the true names of Malachor and Liloth. The archive is staffed by monks who have sworn vows of silence; they communicate through signs and written notation. The collection includes not just scrolls and codices, but captured spirits bound in crystalline vessels, each containing the knowledge of a long-dead scholar. The deeper chambers descend into catacombs where the truly forbidden texts rest, kept behind locks that require blood-sacrifice to open.
The Scholar's Archive of Forbidden Tongu.hiddenInfo: The archive's head keeper is a fugitive researcher from an enemy nation who pretends compliance while secretly copying texts to smuggle out. Certain books in the collection are cursed—their readers age decades in moments, or go mad with visions. The most valuable text, hidden behind seven locked doors, contains the ritual by which the devouring gods can be summoned fully into the mortal world; it is written in a cipher that changes every time someone reads it. Three scholar-networks operate within the archive, all seeking the same forbidden knowledge for different reasons: the priests to summon their gods, a resistance movement to bind and imprison the gods, and a third faction seeking to awaken something else entirely.

Harborside Slave-Pens of the Crimson Row.name: Harborside Slave-Pens of the Crimson Row
Harborside Slave-Pens of the Crimson Row.basicInfo: Along the harbor's southern edge stand massive wooden structures bristling with iron bars: the holding-pens where freshly arrived slaves are processed before sale or assignment. The screams never truly stop. Thousands are crammed into spaces meant for hundreds, chained to one another, with minimal water and food. Disease is rampant; corpses are removed daily. Overseers move through the pens with whips and brands, maintaining brutal order. It is here that resistance is broken, spirits shattered, and humans transformed into property. The smell—layers of human waste, blood, fear-sweat, and putrescence—clings to every surface and lingers long after leaving.
Harborside Slave-Pens of the Crimson Row.hiddenInfo: One of the pen-masters is actually an escaped slave who infiltrated years ago using a false identity; he subtly protects those with useful skills, misdirecting them toward less lethal assignments. The pens' medical officer performs illegal surgeries, grafting tattoos onto slaves' minds through elaborate processes—some markings grant resistance to pain or command-words, others are pure control mechanisms. Disease spreads deliberately in certain pens to thin numbers before they reach price-point; the master-controller admits only those who recover to the auction-block, ensuring only the strongest survive. A network of sympathetic guards communicates escape routes through coded messages scratched into the wooden walls.

The Sunset Marina of Merchant-Princes.name: The Sunset Marina of Merchant-Princes
The Sunset Marina of Merchant-Princes.basicInfo: A crescent harbor where the wealthiest merchant-lords maintain private mooring fields for their personal vessels. The docks here are ornate, decorated with marble and precious woods, guarded obsessively. The yachts anchored here represent fortunes—some ships are moving palaces, equipped with every luxury. Here, the elite of Tharsis conduct their most delicate negotiations: treaties sealed with blood, betrayals orchestrated over wine, marriages arranged between rival houses. The marina operates under its own laws, enforced by private guard-companies loyal only to coin. The water itself seems cleaner here, somehow less corrupted than elsewhere in Tharsis.
The Sunset Marina of Merchant-Princes.hiddenInfo: The marina's administrative building contains a legendary record-vault where copies of every treaty, pact, and blood-oath made in Tharsis for the last three centuries are supposedly kept. A spy-network operates here under the guise of dock-workers and servant-staff, each merchant-prince maintaining paid informants who report on rivals' movements and dealings. The most beautiful yacht in the harbor belongs to a woman who is not human—she may be a construct, a preserved ancient, or something else entirely, but she has made a pact with each of the top five merchant-princes, and her vessel can be seen one night with each in turn, then vanishes for a month before returning.

Castellum Fabius.name: Castellum Fabius
Castellum Fabius.basicInfo: The crumbling ancestral seat of House Fabius sprawls across a hilltop in weathered travertine and moss-greened marble, its bastioned wings half-reclaimed by ivy and time. Once a proud stronghold of the Roman-imperial order, the fortress now exhales melancholy—its great courtyard echoes with the shuffle of aged servants and the whispered prayers of caretakers bound by duty older than memory. Wardstones embedded in its foundations hum with a faint, soporific resonance, and the library within is said to contain rituals inscribed before the current empire drew its first breath.
Castellum Fabius.hiddenInfo: Beneath the fortress, sealed chambers contain the true seat of Fabius power: a binding circle where the ward over the Deep was first wrought. The bloodline maintains a dark secret—one child per generation must drink from a silver ewer filled with water drawn from the sealed places, binding their lifespan to the integrity of the wards. Three wardens have already expired, their deaths hastening the wards' slow decay. The lord of the house grows desperate, seeking external aid or forbidden knowledge to reverse the pact.

Outer Courtyard.description: A vast, windswept plaza of cracked flagstone surrounded by colonnade ruins. Wardstones the size of men stand at cardinal points, carved with warnings in Old Latin. The sound here is wrong—voices echo and repeat as if from some distant chamber, and the light always seems grey, even at midday.

Great Hall.description: A vaulted space of classical proportions, its frescoes peeling into abstract patterns. High-backed chairs face a cold hearth; tapestries depicting forgotten victories hang in tatters. The air is thick and still, heavy with the weight of authority no longer exercised. A spiral staircase descends into shadow.

Library.description: Towering shelves crammed with crumbling codices, many in scripts no one still reads. Dust motes drift through slanted light. A worn desk holds open ledgers recording ritual rites and bloodline genealogies, their margins filled with frantic, recent annotations in a shaking hand.

Eastern Tower.description: A round defensive structure, its lower chambers now used as living quarters by the handful of Fabius servants who have not fled. Spiral stairs climb toward an observation gallery overlooking the Wardlands, where a single warden maintains a watch that has grown more anxious with each passing year.

Cellars Below.description: A descent into older stonework, far older than the fortress above. Cool air rises from depths unseen. Stone channels run along the floor, dry now, carved in geometric patterns that hurt the eye. A sealed bronze door, elaborate with locks that no key fits, bars further passage.

The Sealing Stones of Mortuis.name: The Sealing Stones of Mortuis
The Sealing Stones of Mortuis.basicInfo: A vast circle of worked wardstones rises from a clearing of barren earth, each stone twelve feet tall and carved with binding sigils that have darkened with age into barely-legible runes. The ground here is bare of grass despite the surrounding forest; no animal will graze within a stone's throw, and birds do not sing overhead. A low thrumming emanates from the earth itself, a sound felt more than heard—the sound of something vast, patient, and imprisoned, dreaming beneath ancient chains.
The Sealing Stones of Mortuis.hiddenInfo: This is one of three primary sealing sites where the greatest ward over the Deep was enacted centuries ago. The stones themselves are not just carved—they are inscribed with the names of those who died to power the spell, their essence bound into the stone itself. Recent tremors have widened cracks in the northern stones; priests report that the old names on the western face have begun to glow faintly at night. Worse, something beneath has begun to push back against the sealing, and pilgrims who sleep near the stones report shared nightmares of waking in flooded caverns, their lungs burning with salt water and silt.

The Circle.description: The heart of the sealing site, where twelve wardstones form a perfect geometric ring. The earth is compacted and dark, stained by ceremonies old and new. At the center, a perfectly circular stone basin has been carved, its interior smooth as glass, filled with water so dark it appears black. Touching the water induces vivid, disturbing visions.

The Northern Watch.description: A small wooden shelter, ancient and barely maintained, where a warden keeps perpetual vigil. Inside, a log records tremors, cracks, and aberrations—the last entries in a shaking hand note that 'the pushing grows stronger' and 'something remembers its old hunger.' A brazier burns continuously, fed with herbs that smell of burning hair and copper.

The Pilgrim's Path.description: A winding trail of white stones leading away from the sealing circle through twisted oaks and mist-draped pines. Shrine markers line the path at intervals, each bearing offerings—flowers, coins, sometimes desperate letters written on parchment. The path is worn from countless feet, yet few pilgrims remain in the Wardlands.

The Barrow of the Forgotten Kings.name: The Barrow of the Forgotten Kings
The Barrow of the Forgotten Kings.basicInfo: A vast earthen mound rises from a hollow, its slope cloaked in silver birch and yew. Three stone entrances, each sealed with worked doors of grey granite, mark cardinal passages into the dark. The mound is impossibly old—older than the Fabian house, older perhaps than the civilization that built the sealing ward. Local folk will not approach after dusk; they say the barrow 'breathes' when the sun sets, and those who have entered to rob tombs have emerged aged years in a single night, their minds shattered.
The Barrow of the Forgotten Kings.hiddenInfo: The barrow is not merely a tomb. It is a repository of pre-imperial knowledge, sealed by the ancients who came before Rome brought order to the world. The three kings interred here were not men—or if they were, they were changed by something in the Deep itself. Grave robbers centuries past found cryptic records suggesting the kings 'stood between the waking and the sleeping, guardians of the thin place.' Skeletal remains found in one chamber bore evidence of deliberate ritual modification, bones filed and reshaped into patterns that correspond to the warding sigils. Most troubling: the bones have been moving. Recent expeditions found them arranged differently than in historical records.

The Eastern Door.description: The most recently sealed entrance, its granite door marked with fresh chisel marks from some futile attempt at entry. A chamber beyond holds pottery and funeral goods arranged in obsessive geometric patterns. The air is cool and dry, and smells faintly of myrrh and something else—something mineral and organic, like deep caves and old blood.

The Central Chamber.description: A vast underground space carved from living rock, its ceiling lost to shadow. Three raised sarcophagi of unmarked black stone rest on dais. The carvings on the walls tell a story of descent—figures walking downward, always downward, toward something beneath the earth, and their forms becoming increasingly abstract, until the final figures are unrecognizably strange.

The Western Passage.description: A narrow tunnel descending steeply, its walls water-worn and smooth as glass. Blind white fish swim in pools along the floor—somehow alive in absolute darkness, feeding on nothing visible. The passage reeks of salt and minerals, and at its end, a sealed iron door bars further passage. The seal bears no lock.

Falcidii.name: Falcidii
Falcidii.basicInfo: A modest village of some fifty souls, huddled in a valley where a cold stream winds through stands of birch and alder. Thatched cottages cluster around a stone shrine dedicated to a god whose name no one remembers. The folk here are dour, superstitious, and old—few children are born, and those who are grow up aware of some unspoken dread in the mist-covered hills. The village well runs clear but tastes faintly of copper, and no one drinks from it after dark.
Falcidii.hiddenInfo: Falcidii has slowly depopulated over the past three generations, and no one speaks of why. The truth is whispered only by the oldest: on certain nights when the mist grows thick, things emerge from the deep valleys—not quite flesh, not quite shadow. The village lost an entire family in the winter of 203 AUC, found in their cottage arranged in a circle around their hearth, their skin grey as slate. The magistrate from Castellum Fabius came and forbade discussion. Now the village is sustained by a handful of families too old or too stubborn to leave, and by the strange charity of House Fabius, which sends food and occasionally young wards to 'teach' the village children lore that no child should know.

The Shrine of the Unnamed.name: The Shrine of the Unnamed
The Shrine of the Unnamed.basicInfo: A small stone temple, pre-Roman in origin, stands in a clearing where five roads converge. Its altars are worn smooth by countless hands and countless centuries. No priest tends it, yet offerings appear constantly—flowers, food, coins left by desperate pilgrims seeking intervention for illness, barrenness, or visions that plague their sleep. The shrine's stone is pale, almost luminescent in moonlight, and the air around it carries the scent of myrrh and something acrid, like singed rope.
The Shrine of the Unnamed.hiddenInfo: The shrine was built atop a deep spring that emerges from sources far below. The water has strange properties—those who drink it experience visions of 'what lies beneath,' fragmentary memories that are not their own. Offerings of coins that rest in the spring water become warm, sometimes hot enough to burn. House Fabius forbids access to the shrine's depths, but locals have for generations dropped questions written on parchment into its waters, seeking answers from whatever dwells there. Some believe the shrine is a mouth of the Deep itself, a place where its ancient consciousness can whisper back.

Dun Caustus.name: Dun Caustus
Dun Caustus.basicInfo: A ruined watchtower stands sentinel on a stark ridge, its walls of dark basalt tumbling into moss and lichen. The tower was meant to command the northern approaches to the Wardlands, but it has been abandoned for generations. Its upper stories have collapsed, and what remains is a hollow shell where wind shrieks through empty windows. Local herders claim to see figures walking the ridge at night, keeping watch over nothing, and that the wind through the ruin often carries words—warnings, repeated endlessly in a language no living soul speaks.
Dun Caustus.hiddenInfo: Dun Caustus was abandoned after the garrison discovered something in the sub-chambers below the tower—artifact evidence of a civilization that predates Rome by millennia, and records suggesting the tower itself was built atop a 'weak point' in the sealing. The last commander's report, found in Castellum Fabius archives, is a single line: 'The seal shows cracks. We dare not descend further.' The tower hums with a frequency that troubles the blood, and those who spend a night in its shadow wake with nosebleeds and gaps in their memory.

Palus Obscura.name: Palus Obscura
Palus Obscura.basicInfo: A sprawling fen stretches across low ground in the heart of the Wardlands, a maze of brackish water, sphagnum moss, and twisted willows whose branches arch into strange, bone-like formations. Mist rises perpetually from the water, so thick that even in daylight, visibility extends only a few paces. Nothing hunts here, but something watches. The very air feels pregnant with attention—a weight that increases the longer one remains within the marsh's boundaries.
