The main settlement sits atop the works: a chaotic sprawl of ore-processing stations, slag heaps that poison the air, and ramshackle barracks where the miners sleep in shifts. The sound of hammers never ceases. The ground trembles with the work of a thousand hands, and the acrid smoke from the smelters darkens the sky for leagues.
The Deepdelve Mines.hiddenInfo: The Deepdelve's ore is not merely iron—layered within the deepest veins is a luminous metal that the miners call 'Tharsi gold,' prized by furycraft smiths for its conductivity of elemental workings. Crassus sends much of it directly to Tharsis via diplomatic caravan, cementing its financial dependence on their funds. The mine manager, a cruel overseer called Maxidus, keeps meticulous tallies of ore production and worker mortality—the latter is remarkably low, suggesting that failed miners are disposed of quietly rather than recorded. A slave uprising was brutally crushed three winters past; bodies were left in the deepest shafts as a warning. Some miners whisper of strange luminescence in the lowest shafts and of voices singing in a tongue no living thing should know.

Upper Workings.description: Open-air mining terraces where pick-wielders scrape at exposed seams of red stone. Dust rises in clouds. Overseers on horseback patrol between the work gangs. Crude wooden scaffolding creaks precariously.

Processing Station.description: A thunderous complex of stamps, furnaces, and troughs where raw ore is crushed, smelted, and refined. Sweat-slicked workers tend molten vats. The heat is intense enough to buckle wood. The smell of burning metal and charcoal is acrid and choking.

Barracks Settlement.description: A ramshackle village of canvas tents and timber huts where miners sleep and eat. A few stone buildings house the overseer's office and a makeshift shrine. The smell is of unwashed bodies, stale bread, and urine. Tension hangs heavy as the miners eye each other with suspicion born of scarcity.

Shaft Access.description: A wide vertical opening descending into shadow, with timber scaffolding and rope pulleys for hauling ore and lowering workers. The sound of picks echoes from the depths. Cold air rises from below, carrying the smell of stone and something else—something faintly sweet and wrong.

The Mercenary Muster.name: The Mercenary Muster
The Mercenary Muster.basicInfo: A sprawling military encampment on the dusty plains west of the Crassan seat, the Muster serves as the holding ground and training ground for House Crassus's hired blades—tough, cynical soldiers of fortune bound by coin and contract rather than any loyalty to House or realm. Tents arranged in martial rows, exercise yards where men-at-arms drill with sword and spear, and a central pavilion where captains and quartermasters negotiate wages and contracts. The sound of combat training and the jingle of armor fills the air. Crassus maintains perhaps three thousand soldiers here, a formidable force kept constantly ready and constantly on edge by irregular payment and the threat of wage-cuts for insubordination.
The Mercenary Muster.hiddenInfo: The Muster is, in truth, Crassus's insurance policy against Tharsi overreach. Though officially contracted to Crassus for local defense, many of these soldiers are secretly on Tharsi payroll as well—a fact carefully hidden from House officers. When Crassus needs to appear compliant to Tharsis, the Muster pulls soldiers for 'loan' to assist southern interests; when Crassus needs to maintain independence, the Muster can field a force that rivals regional powers. The quartermaster, a scarred old campaigner named Grendus, keeps two sets of contract ledgers—one true, one false. He has been bribed by every faction with interest in the Holdings. Desertion rates are high; those caught are executed publicly to maintain discipline.

Training Grounds.description: Open dirt yards where soldiers drill and spar, their shouts and the crack of wooden practice swords filling the air. Officers on horseback observe and shout corrections. The earth is beaten hard and stained with old blood.

Barracks Rows.description: Hundreds of canvas tents arranged in precise lines, each housing four to six soldiers. Smoke from small cooking fires drifts across the encampment. The smell of too many bodies, cooking stew, and horse dung is overwhelming. Small gambling circles huddle outside tents.

Central Pavilion.description: A large tent of heavy canvas striped with the Crassan colors, housing tables where officers negotiate contracts and captains report to commanders. Maps of the region are pinned to boards. The air is thick with the smell of ale and ink.

Armory and Stores.description: A fortified timber structure housing weapons, armor, and supplies. Grim-faced soldiers inventory weapons and armor constantly. The smell of oil and metal dominates. A locked back room holds the quartermaster's ledgers.

Tharsis Reach Cove.name: Tharsis Reach Cove
Tharsis Reach Cove.basicInfo: Hidden among the rocky inlets of the southern coast, where three streams converge into the sea, the Cove is Crassus's best-kept secret—a smugglers' haven serving as the clandestine entrepôt for goods and correspondence flowing between House Crassus and its Tharsi paymasters. A ramshackle settlement of weathered docks, rough shelters, and carefully concealed warehouses line the shore. Ships arrive at night, keels unmarked, anchors dropped in the shallows. Crates are loaded and unloaded with swift, practiced efficiency. The cove stinks of brine, fish, and the resinous smoke of signal fires. Few outsiders know it exists; those who stumble upon it do not return.
Tharsis Reach Cove.hiddenInfo: The Cove is where House Crassus receives not just coin but strategic intelligence from Tharsis—maps of rival territories, intelligence on movements of other Houses, even rumors of approaching war. In return, Crassus sends ore, slaves for Tharsi labor camps, and most dangerously, information on the other Houses' positions and movements, a betrayal so profound that if discovered would bring unified House vengeance upon Crassus. The master of the Cove is a scarred woman named Peloria who answers only to the Crassan legate and to one Tharsi contact known only as 'The Merchant'—no one knows his true identity. A hidden vault beneath the principal warehouse holds encrypted records of every transaction.

The Debtors' Warren.name: The Debtors' Warren
The Debtors' Warren.basicInfo: A sprawling shantytown packed into a maze of narrow alleys and ramshackle dwellings built from salvaged wood and canvas, the Warren is where Crassus houses those who owe debts they cannot pay—failed merchants, gamblers, soldiers who lost limbs and wages, families whose fathers drowned. Overcrowded, disease-prone, and perpetually hungry, the Warren is a managed slum where Crassus can legally hold debtors until they work off their obligations or perish. Work-gangs are assembled daily in the central square; those selected are promised a percentage of wages toward their debt. Those unselected receive only a thin gruel. Desperation hangs heavier than the stench of unwashed humanity.
The Debtors' Warren.hiddenInfo: The Warren is a tool of social control as much as debt collection. Crassus deliberately keeps the Warren miserable and dangerous to ensure that debtors have every incentive to vanish into the borderlands or accept any labor contract offered. Those who attempt escape are hunted by Crassan enforcers; upon recapture, their debt is doubled and they are sold to the mines or the galleys. A secret network of Warren residents trades in contraband—escape routes for a fee, poison for those too broken to work, and whispered information about Crassus's dealings that rivals would pay dearly to hear.

The Toll-Fort of Arx Viae.name: The Toll-Fort of Arx Viae
The Toll-Fort of Arx Viae.basicInfo: A squared stone fortress built athwart the ancient trade road that winds through the Holdings, connecting the war-coast to the Tharsis gates, the Toll-Fort is Crassus's chosen tax-point and customs station. Stone walls rise three stories; guard towers flank the road itself. Every caravan, merchant, and traveler must pass through the gates where guards levy tariffs, conduct searches, and catalog goods. A garrison of perhaps two hundred soldiers rotates duty; they are disciplined and professional, trained to smile whilst they empty purses. Beyond the gate, the landscape to north and south is deliberately left open—any attempt to circumvent the toll brings swift cavalry response.
The Toll-Fort of Arx Viae.hiddenInfo: The Toll-Fort collects more than tariffs. Each merchant's goods and route are catalogued in detail, providing Crassus with a perfect intelligence network of trade flows and merchant movements. Certain 'inspection officers' record private letters and correspondence, selling copies to whoever pays—rival Houses, the Crown's agents, even Tharsi scouts. A corrupt captain named Vexus runs a secondary operation: merchants who pay 'special fees' can bypass normal taxation and have their goods marked as Crassus-certified for sale at inflated prices throughout the realm. Most disturbingly, the Fort sits atop an older structure—ruins of a tower that predates House Crassus, marked with symbols no scholar can decipher.

The Iron Foundry of Sanguis.name: The Iron Foundry of Sanguis
The Iron Foundry of Sanguis.basicInfo: A massive industrial complex sprawling across the eastern edge of the Holdings' main settlement, Sanguis is both furnace and forge where ore from the Deepdelve is transformed into weapons and tools. Tremendous stone kilns roar day and night, fed by slaves and paid workers in shifts. The clang of hammers on anvils is constant and hypnotic. Molten metal flows in channels carved into the floor; the heat is so intense that the stone itself glows. Master smiths oversee teams of apprentices and laborers, producing everything from common nails to elaborate mail-shirts for the Muster. The air shimmers with heat-haze and reeking smoke.
The Iron Foundry of Sanguis.hiddenInfo: Sanguis is where Crassus experiments with weaponcraft infused with furycraft—swords quenched in water drawn from deep wells that supposedly contain elemental echoes, armor etched with symbols meant to attune to fire or earth. The results are inconsistent and often unstable, but those that succeed are sold at obscene prices or sent to Tharsis as tribute. A master smith named Ultor has grown wealthy beyond measure from secret commissions; he crafts items of terrible power for clients whose gold arrives in unmarked pouches. Bodies occasionally disappear from the foundry—accidents, officially, though some suspect failed furycraft-working victims are simply melted into the next batch of steel.

The Silver Tongue Row.name: The Silver Tongue Row
The Silver Tongue Row.basicInfo: An elegant street of marble-fronted counting-houses, moneylenders' offices, and merchant-bankers' establishments nestled within the main settlement's wealthy quarter, the Row is where serious wealth is transacted and leveraged. Gold-lettered signboards announce rates of exchange, loan terms, and investment opportunities. Cloaked figures pass frequently between offices. Armed guards stand discreetly at doorways. The air smells of beeswax candles, fine leather, and the faint metallic scent of coin. Here, fortunes are made and destroyed in a single conversation.
The Silver Tongue Row.hiddenInfo: The Row is where Crassus brokers the most delicate financial arrangements—loans to rivals with hidden interest penalties, investments in competitors designed to bleed them dry, and exchanges with Tharsis that disguise the flow of forbidden goods as legitimate trade. Certain establishments are fronts for intelligence brokers; a moneylender named Corax trades in secrets as readily as coin. Clients who default on major loans don't merely lose coin—they lose everything, including sometimes their lives. There are whispers of a hidden society among the moneylenders, sworn to maintain secrecy and mutual profit at any cost.

The Slave Market of Catena.name: The Slave Market of Catena
The Slave Market of Catena.basicInfo: An open-air plaza surrounded by auction-blocks, holding pens, and record-offices, the Market operates three times per week under Crassus's official sanction. Chained men, women, and children are displayed for purchase by merchants, miners, farmholders, and contractors seeking labor. Auctioneers call out prices whilst inspectors certify health and fitness. The sound is a sickening cacophony of chains, weeping, and commercial banter. The smell is of too many unwashed bodies in close confinement, human waste, and the iron-sharp stench of fear. Crassus profits handsomely from every transaction, and the Market is one of the most heavily guarded locations in the Holdings.
The Slave Market of Catena.hiddenInfo: Catena is not merely a market but a sorting station for Tharsi interests. Certain slaves are marked secretly for Tharsi buyers and transported south; others are held for ransom or blackmail—the market processes dozens of kidnapped children of moderate Houses and merchant families, held until ransoms arrive. A notary named Caesus maintains falsified records that list slaves as 'deceased' or 'sold to unknown merchants,' covering the tracks of those diverted to Tharsis. Slaves who recognize valuable information are kept alive and interrogated, then sold to the highest bidder rather than simply processed. There is a network of slave-rebels and sympathizers who maintain safehouses throughout the Holdings, attempting to spirit runaways toward the Free Cities.

The Granite Vault of Crassus Prime.name: The Granite Vault of Crassus Prime
The Granite Vault of Crassus Prime.basicInfo: Built into the bedrock of the most heavily fortified hill in the Holdings, Crassus Prime is the ultimate treasury and sanctuary for House Crassus in times of siege or civil turmoil. A massive rounded dome of fitted stone, nearly windowless, with a single iron gate barred from the inside. The entrance is guarded by an elite company of Crassus soldiers sworn to die rather than permit entry by force. The interior is rumored to contain not merely the House's treasury but also artifacts of power, ancient texts, and relics from an age before the current Houses rose to prominence. Few have ever entered; none have ever spoken of what they saw within.
The Granite Vault of Crassus Prime.hiddenInfo: Crassus Prime is sealed with furycraft-worked locks that respond only to bloodline and spoken ritual; no force alone can breach it. Beyond the vast chambers of stacked coin and jewels lies a deeper sanctum, sealed behind a second set of wards, where Crassus keeps records of every blackmail victim, every illegal pact, and every betrayal committed in the House's name—insurance against catastrophe and leverage for survival. Some say a skeleton still hangs in chains within the deepest vault, a traitor from centuries past left as eternal warning. Access to the records is limited to perhaps three individuals alive at any time; the current custodian has not been outside the vault in five years.

Tharsos the Eternal.name: Tharsos the Eternal
Tharsos the Eternal.basicInfo: The great blood-temple-city sprawls across a black basalt plain, its obsidian towers drinking the sun. Ritual chanting echoes ceaselessly from open-air amphitheaters where ten thousand devotees sway in synchronized frenzy. The Crimson Canal—a channel of actual blood from daily sacrifices—snakes through the city's heart, painting the streets in visceral testimony. Priests in scarlet robes glide between columned shrines, their shadows impossibly long. The air tastes of iron, cinnamon-heavy incense, and something burnt beyond recognition. Pilgrims arrive broken and leave exalted, or don't leave at all.
Tharsos the Eternal.hiddenInfo: Beneath the city lie the Bone Crypts—catacombs where failed sacrifices are entombed in honeycomb stone chambers. The blood-priests harvest their bones for ritual implements. The city's true power lies in the Scarlet Sanctum, a hidden inner temple where the high priesthood conducts auguries by reading entrails of creatures born in captivity—creatures that whisper prophecies in backwards human speech before death. The Crimson Canal's flow is regulated by a system of locks controlled from a submerged chamber only the Archpriest knows how to navigate. Pilgrims who vanish are often conscripted into the Eternal Choir, chained singers who perform for eternity in the Grand Amphitheater's deepest cavern.

Grand Amphitheater.description: A massive open-air arena carved into living black stone, capable of holding fifty thousand screaming devotees. The central blood-pit glimmers with a permanent sheen of crimson that never seems to dry. Soaring pillars carved as skeletal giants frame the space. The air vibrates with constant chanting—low, rhythmic, hypnotic.

The Crimson Canal.description: The arterial channel that flows through the city's spine, running blood-warm and perpetually red. Stone bridges cross it at regular intervals. The far banks are lined with smaller shrines where pilgrims kneel to drink the waters, believing it grants visions. The smell here is overwhelming—copper, decay, and sweet rot.

Shrine of Malachor.description: A towering temple dedicated to the devouring god, its walls carved with thousands of mouths—some smiling, some screaming, all eternally open. Within, the priesthood performs hourly rituals, offering flowers and birds to ever-hungry altar-stones.

Shrine of Liloth.description: The goddess of endings is honored in this inverted temple, where the floor slopes downward to a central pit filled with ash and bone. Worshippers descend in candlelit processions, leaving offerings of woven hair and shed skin.

The Priest-Gardens.description: Grotesque botanical gardens where flesh-flowers bloom red and writhing, where vines seem to whisper, where the priests cultivate plants used in both ecstatic potions and lethal poisons. The air shimmers with hallucinogenic pollen.

The Bone-Smith's Quarter.description: Where the city's craftsmen hammer bone and obsidian into implements of ritual and war. The rhythm of thousands of strikes creates a constant, maddening percussion. The streets here are slick with bone-dust that catches light like stars.

The High Pyre-Sanctum.name: The High Pyre-Sanctum
The High Pyre-Sanctum.basicInfo: Atop a blood-red mesa crowned with obsidian spires stands the Sanctum—a ring of thirteen towering pyres that burn without fuel and without ceasing. The flames dance purple and green, fed by god-magic and something darker. Smoke billows in columns thick enough to see from the coast, creating a permanent twilight beneath clouds of ash. The ground itself radiates heat. Pilgrims climb the mesa in processions that take three days; many collapse before reaching the summit. Those who do arrive often choose to walk into the flames, screaming hymns of ecstasy.
The High Pyre-Sanctum.hiddenInfo: The pyres are fueled by the blood-curse itself—they consume the lingering essence of sacrificed victims, burning them alive and dead simultaneously. Each pyre corresponds to a different god-aspect, and the high priests read prophecy in the flames' dance. The Sanctum contains a hidden chamber within the central pyre where the priesthood stores crystallized blood—ancient sacrifice condensed into glowing amber shards that grant visions of divine wrath. Climbers who disappear partway up the mesa don't die—they're inducted into the Flame-Walkers, ascetics who sleep in shallow caves carved into the rock and serve as living conduits between the mortal realm and the gods' hunger. The very stone of the mesa whispers with voices of the consumed.

The Pilgrim Path.description: A wind-carved stone track spiraling up the mesa's eastern face, marked with prayer-stones and the bones of the faithful who fell short. Hand-holds are worn smooth by centuries of desperate climbing. Prayer-markers left behind flutter in the superheated air.

The Resting Grottoes.description: Shallow caves carved into the cliff face at intervals, where pilgrims rest or expire. Some contain offerings—coins, locks of hair, shredded garments. The walls glow faintly in darkness.

The Summit Ring.description: The plateau atop the mesa, ringed by thirteen eternal pyres each forty feet high. The heat makes vision shimmer. At the ring's center stands a stone dais where the high priests conduct the Great Summoning once yearly—a ritual that allegedly opens the lips of the gods themselves.

The Flame-Circle.description: The space between the pyres themselves, where the ground is black glass fused from stone and heat. Walking here requires ritual protection or madness claims the body within minutes. Sacrificial offerings are left here to be consumed by the eternal fire.

The Priest-Seminary of Scarlet Thorns.name: The Priest-Seminary of Scarlet Thorns
The Priest-Seminary of Scarlet Thorns.basicInfo: A sprawling monastery carved into the bones of a dead mountain, where future blood-priests undergo their vile education. Stone courtyards echo with the screams of acolytes undergoing transformation rituals. The seminary's whitewashed walls are perpetually stained rust-brown despite obsessive scrubbing. Gardens of thorned flowers bloom red and hungry. The priesthood here practices flagellation, ordeal-magic, and the art of reading divinity in the suffering of small creatures. Aspirants arrive hopeful and emerge either broken or transcendent—occasionally both.
The Priest-Seminary of Scarlet Thorns.hiddenInfo: The seminary's true curriculum teaches forbidden blood-weaving—the ability to bind the life-essence of others into blood-runes inscribed on the body. Successful students bear these marks upon their hearts, invisible to normal sight but sensed by other blood-priests. In the seminary's deepest crypt lies the Chamber of Echoes, where the voices of all students who've died in training persist as disembodied whispers, teaching newcomers in dreams. The high master of the seminary, an ancient priest named Valdris, has performed the Eternal Binding on himself seventeen times—his body is a patchwork of scars and tattoos, each one a minor god bound to his flesh. The most dangerous secret: the seminary doesn't teach cruelty—it teaches transcendence through sacrifice. Students gradually realize the rituals are teaching them to love their own pain.

The Initiate Courtyards.description: Open-air stone courtyards where new acolytes learn basic rituals and combat forms. The ground is cracked and stained. Blood-sigils are painted fresh each dawn. The smell of pain and dedication permeates.

The Hall of Thorns.description: A vaulted library and meditation chamber where advanced students study the texts of blood-priesthood. Shelves line the walls, filled with grimoires bound in scaled leather. The air is thick with incense-smoke that induces visions.

The Ordeal Chambers.description: A series of sealed stone rooms where acolytes undergo trials designed to break and remake them. The air carries the echo of countless screams. Bloodstains are carved deep into the stone floors—this is old, old blood.

The Chamber of Transformation.description: The seminary's heart, where successful acolytes receive the blood-mark. A circular room with a central altar and thirteen priest-witnesses. The walls are smooth black stone and perfectly soundproof. What occurs here remains secret even to those who survive it.

The Crimson Amphitheater.name: The Crimson Amphitheater
The Crimson Amphitheater.basicInfo: An ancient arena built into a natural depression, its tiered stone seats stained the color of rust. This is where the games occur—not games of sport, but of sacrifice. Condemned criminals, sacrificial animals, and the occasionally willing victim face one another and the will of the crowd. The noise here is deafening: thousands chanting in unison, drums thundering like divine heartbeats, the wet sounds of violence echoing from the pit. After each game, the entire amphitheater must be ritually cleansed, priests walking the seats with smoking braziers, chanting prayers that sound more like accusations.
The Crimson Amphitheater.hiddenInfo: The amphitheater's true purpose is sacrifice evaluation—the priests believe that the way a creature dies reveals divine truth. They study the patterns of blood-spray, the angle of the final breath, the death-words of the condemned. From these signs, they divine prophecy and the gods' current hunger. The deepest chamber beneath the arena is a bone-cathedral where the skulls of all who've died in the amphitheater are stacked in geometric patterns meant to align with the stars. Those patterns shift subtly each year, and the priesthood interprets these shifts as cosmic mandates. The sand of the arena itself is mixed with the ashes of the ancient dead—stepping on it is said to grant communion with the sacrificed.

Pylae, the Dread-Pilgrim Town.name: Pylae, the Dread-Pilgrim Town
Pylae, the Dread-Pilgrim Town.basicInfo: A sprawling settlement of pilgrims, penitents, and the desperate seeking redemption or oblivion. Streets twist in confusing spirals leading toward a central temple-shrine. The town smells of sweat, hope, and desperation. Pilgrims wear ash and rags, many self-flagellated or bearing ritual scars. Vendors sell cheap amulets, pain-wines, and bloodstones promising divine favor. Nightly, collective prayers echo through the cramped streets—thousands of voices begging the gods for mercy they suspect won't come. The town has no proper law; the blood-priests' will is the only order.
Pylae, the Dread-Pilgrim Town.hiddenInfo: Pylae is not a destination—it's a sieve. Pilgrims arrive seeking salvation but are gradually identified by the priesthood's agents as either worthy sacrifices or potential acolytes. Those selected simply disappear from the town, taken in the night. The town's taverns are all run by former priests who've been cast out, and they engage in a quiet resistance—spreading word that the blood-gods are not real, only ancient lies perpetuated by the priesthood's hunger for power. These resistance members mark sympathetic pilgrims with invisible inks, leading them to hidden exits that bypass the temple entirely. The town sits on a mass grave—when the priesthood needs fresh bodies for dark rituals, Pylae is where they harvest them, making the disappearances seem like divine ascension.

The Half-Drowned Shrine of Sorrows.name: The Half-Drowned Shrine of Sorrows
The Half-Drowned Shrine of Sorrows.basicInfo: A temple dedicated to a forgotten god—or perhaps a god of forgetting—that has partially sunk into a brackish tidal lagoon. The upper half of the structure rises above the murky water, its stones bearded with algae and decay. A narrow causeway of slippery stones leads to the shrine's entrance. Inside, water seeps through the walls, creating perpetual dripping sounds that echo through the chamber. Pilgrims wade through the flooded sections, water rising to their waists, seeking the inner sanctum where a statue of the god—its features worn away by centuries—stands half-submerged.
The Half-Drowned Shrine of Sorrows.hiddenInfo: The shrine is sinking deliberately—a priest once performed a ritual binding the god-statue to the tides, causing the structure to slowly submerge. The goal is to eventually have the statue completely underwater, where pilgrims must dive to touch it, creating a literal descent into oblivion to commune with godhood. Those who dive find that time moves strangely underwater; some return after minutes claiming they've spent years in communion. Some don't return at all, their bodies found much later washed onto distant shores. The statue itself whispers—people who touch it often become obsessed with returning, eventually wading deeper and deeper until the tides take them.

Ghast-Market of the Flesh-Vendors.name: Ghast-Market of the Flesh-Vendors
Ghast-Market of the Flesh-Vendors.basicInfo: An open-air market carved into the side of a cliff overlooking the coast, where flesh-merchants sell wares that blur the line between legitimate trade and absolute nightmare. Here you find exotic animals—and less exotic prisoners—hung in chains for inspection. Meat-vendors sell cuts labeled with euphemisms that don't quite hide their true origins. The market is perpetually alive with haggling, the screams of livestock (or worse), and the stench of death rendered mundane. Priests walk among the stalls, selecting items for ceremonial purposes. The market operates under the priesthood's blessing, sanctioned commerce between sacrifice and salvation.
Ghast-Market of the Flesh-Vendors.hiddenInfo: The market is where the priesthood sources their sacrificial victims, but it's also a black market for those seeking to flee Tharsis's dominion. Certain vendors secretly sell maps, poisons, and documents of passage to those with coin and courage. There's a hidden network of traders who use the market's chaos as cover to smuggle people northward. The deepest caverns beneath the market contain a slave-trade operation that the priesthood officially denies but tacitly supports—some victims are sold rather than sacrificed, generating income for the temple coffers. The most-guarded secret: certain flesh on sale comes from Tharsis's own dead priests, their bodies rendered into saleable product after ritual consumption ceremonies.

The Oracle's Cavern.name: The Oracle's Cavern
The Oracle's Cavern.basicInfo: A vast cavern system carved into volcanic rock, where the air shimmers with heat and noxious vapors rise from cracks in the earth. Pilgrims descend into darkness guided by priestess-attendants, entering a cathedral of stone formations that seem almost alive in their organic wrongness. At the cavern's deepest point sits the Oracle—a withered figure chained to a stone throne, barely more than bone and papery skin. She speaks in voices not her own, prophecies tumbling from her lips in a stream of bloodied words. Those seeking answers must drink from the sacred pool beneath her throne, waters that taste of iron and revelation.
The Oracle's Cavern.hiddenInfo: The Oracle has been in her throne for over a century, kept alive through blood-rituals and god-magic that borders on immortality. She was once a free priestess who drank too deeply of prophecy and became addicted to the visions. The priesthood now deliberately feeds her the blood of seers to enhance her channeling ability. Those who drink from her sacred pool don't merely see prophecy—they become infected with her gift, slowly going mad as visions plague them every night. The priesthood believes this madness is the god-touch becoming permanent in the blood-drinker's veins. Some of those driven mad by the Oracle's waters have become quite useful to the priesthood as living vessels for prophecy, unable to refuse divine commands.

The Charnel-Grounds of Ascension.name: The Charnel-Grounds of Ascension
The Charnel-Grounds of Ascension.basicInfo: A sprawling necropolis where the honored dead are left exposed upon stone platforms, their bodies decomposing under the open sky as vultures and priests both descend to claim their portions. This is not a burial ground—this is a processing facility. The bones that remain after the birds have fed are collected and transported to the temples for ritual use. The ground itself is white with scattered remains. Pilgrims walk the platforms seeking bones of their ancestors, believing touch with the ancestral dead grants blessing. The priesthood moves among the bodies, singing dirges that sound suspiciously like recipes.
The Charnel-Grounds of Ascension.hiddenInfo: The charnel-grounds are where forbidden ceremonies occur in darkness—the priesthood conducts rituals binding the spirits of the dead into bone-fetishes, creating phylacteries of captured souls. These bound spirits are used as bargaining chips with darker forces, traded to inhuman entities in exchange for power. The bones collected here are carefully documented; the priesthood maintains records of whose essence resides in which bone. Certain deceased priests' remains are never permitted to decompose—they're preserved and animated through blood-magic, becoming the priesthood's greatest secrets: shambling servants that carry out tasks the living priesthood cannot soil their hands with. The vultures that feed here are no longer natural—they're god-touched, extensions of Malachor's hunger, and they remember the taste of specific deaths.

Thassala, the Cowed Fishing Village.name: Thassala, the Cowed Fishing Village
Thassala, the Cowed Fishing Village.basicInfo: A miserable fishing settlement pressed against the coast, where the priesthood's shadow falls perpetually across thatched roofs. The villagers are gaunt, their eyes hollow with generations of exploitation. Each boat that goes out must leave an offering of its finest catch for the temples. Each child born is tested by the priests, those with 'the gift' taken for the seminaries. The village has no name in its own language—the priesthood named it centuries ago and the original name has been forgotten. Here, the apocalypse wears the face of routine.
Thassala, the Cowed Fishing Village.hiddenInfo: Thassala was once a place of genuine faith in the blood-gods, but that faith died generations ago, replaced by fear and habit. The priesthood maintains control through the village's economic dependence—the priests control the salting-houses where fish are preserved, the distribution networks, the grain-stores. No family can feed itself without the priesthood's permission. Beneath the village lies a smuggler's network that uses the fishing routes as cover, moving refugees and escaped slaves to free lands. The village headwoman secretly leads this network, appearing cowed while actively betraying the priesthood's interests. The children taken for the seminaries are systematically abused, and some return broken; the priesthood spreads the story that they've been 'elevated' when they're actually trauma-damaged beyond repair.

The Aureus Harbor.name: The Aureus Harbor
The Aureus Harbor.basicInfo: The beating heart of Tharsis's mercantile dominion sprawls across the coastline like a gilded amphitheater of commerce. Thousands of vessels crowd its channels—merchant galleys laden with silk and spice, sleek corsair dhows, heavy-laden grain barges—their masts a forest of amber and cedar. The harbor reeks of salt, tar, and exotic perfumes, underlaid with the acrid stench of the slave-pens. Quays of pale marble gleam with merchant princes' wealth, where bankers calculate fortunes and brokers negotiate empires. The skyline bristles with warehouses of terraced stone, their roofs crowned with gilded weathervanes. By night, ten thousand lamps cast the water into molten gold, and the sound of carousing drifts from a thousand pleasure-boats anchored offshore.
The Aureus Harbor.hiddenInfo: Beneath the marble quays lies a network of smugglers' tunnels carved centuries ago, now controlled by a shadow-guild that answers to no prince. The grandest merchant vessels carry false manifests—their 'spice' holds are packed with forbidden weapons and heretical texts bound for the blood-priests' enemies. A rotting slave-ship lies deliberately sunk in the harbor's deepest channel, its bones a warning and a secret tomb. The most prestigious banking-houses operate a silent auction of enslaved craftspeople with rare talents, sold privately to the highest bidders. The merchants themselves fear the awakening of something vast in the deeps—sailors speak of lights that move contrary to the currents.

The Grand Quays.description: Where the largest merchant galleys dock and imperial customs are administered. Pristine marble platforms, canopies of crimson silk, and white-uniformed customs-masters overseeing the flow of cargo. This is theater—the display of Tharsis's magnificence.

The Warehouse Quarter.description: Labyrinthine stacks of stone warehouses, each three stories tall and guarded by armed retainers. The air is thick with incense, cedar, and the stink of stored grain rotting at the foundations. Merchants and their agents perpetually haggle over inventory.

The Slave Pens.description: A sprawl of stone corrals and wooden cages open to the sky, where humanity is warehoused before sale. The sound is overwhelming—weeping, prayers, curses in a hundred tongues. Overseers with whips move between the pens. The stench is almost physical. This is where decadence births cruelty.

The Brokers' Esplanade.description: An open plaza of polished black stone where merchants, factors, and trade-lords congregate to strike bargains. Elaborate pavilions with scribes record contracts. The air crackles with the intensity of fortunes changing hands by the hour.

The Banking Halls.description: A cluster of fortified temples to wealth—marble facades inscribed with the names of ancient banking families. Within, ledgers record every debt, every transaction, every secret arrangement. Guards are numerous and cold-eyed.

The Pleasure Docks.description: Where ornate pleasure-barges and floating pavilions cluster, their decks strung with colored lanterns and draped in silks. Laughter and music drift from them, mingling with the scent of wine and perfume. This is where those with coin seek to forget the ugliness of their fortunes.

The Auction House.description: A vast amphitheater of white stone with tiered seating facing a raised platform. Here the highest-value goods—and the most prized human chattels—are sold to the merchant-princes. The air tastes of blood and power.

The Radiant Palace.name: The Radiant Palace
The Radiant Palace.basicInfo: Ascending the terraced gardens of marble and gold, the private seat of House Aurelius crowns the city like a jeweled crown. The palace is a labyrinth of wings, atriums, and towers, each one more sumptuous than the last. Fountains of rose-scented water cascade through courtyards where enslaved dancers entertain during feasts. The walls are inlaid with lapis lazuli and flecks of actual gold, and every chamber is hung with tapestries woven from the rarest silks of the far East. The garden contains plants that bloom only under impossible conditions—rumored to be tended by someone who understands secrets not meant for mortals.
The Radiant Palace.hiddenInfo: The palace's deepest vaults contain not merely gold, but artifacts stolen from the blood-priests' temples centuries ago—objects that hum with terrible power when the moon darkens. The current prince-patriarch has begun hearing voices from the oldest chambers, urging him to 'reclaim what the priests have withheld.' His family members are dying of inexplicable maladies, their skin turning translucent as though all blood were draining inward. In the private gardens, there is a locked iron door to which no one holds the key—it opens only when certain bloodlines approach. The prince's personal attendants include a woman whose reflection does not match her movements.

The Gilded Reception Halls.description: Where visiting dignitaries are received with calculated grandeur. Soaring ceilings painted with murals of Tharsis's 'glorious past,' thick carpets of woven gold thread, and servants trained to anticipate every need before it is spoken.

The Marble Atrium.description: A central space of dizzying scale, where orchestras perform beneath a dome of polished rock crystal that refracts sunlight into rainbows. A shallow pool reflects the sky, and the air is eternally cool and scented with jasmine.

The Feasting Halls.description: Where the prince-patriarch hosts his legendary banquets. Tables of dark wood are laden with delicacies from across the world. The kitchens are hidden but their output is magnificent—and those who refuse to eat what is served sometimes vanish before the next course.

The Winter Gardens.description: Enclosed terraces of hot-stone and rare plants that should not bloom in this climate. Enslaved gardeners tend them obsessively. The air is thick with humidity and the smell of earth, competing with jasmine and nightshade.

The Treasury Vaults.description: Beneath the palace, chambers lined with gold and guarded by mechanisms that are part engineering and part something else. The walls themselves seem to resist intrusion, becoming harder the closer one gets to the deepest chamber.

The Locked Door.description: Deep in the private gardens, an iron portal encrusted with symbols that hurt the eyes to look at. No lock is visible, yet none have ever opened it. Sometimes sounds emerge from beyond—not quite speech, not quite music.

The Crimson Bazaar.name: The Crimson Bazaar
The Crimson Bazaar.basicInfo: An open-air market that sprawls across three interconnected plazas, where every conceivable spice, fabric, jewel, and forbidden thing is bought and sold. The air shimmers with heat and incense. Merchant stalls with crimson awnings create a labyrinth of narrow passages where sellers hawk their wares with operatic passion. The silks of the Eastern kingdoms are draped from every surface; saffron spills from overturned jars in rivers of gold; slave-handlers move through the crowd leading chains of captives to waiting buyers. The sound is a cacophony of languages, haggling, music, and beneath it all, the occasional crack of a whip.
The Crimson Bazaar.hiddenInfo: The Bazaar sits atop the ruins of a much older market from the blood-priests' era, and certain merchants report finding ancient coins and jewelry in their stalls—objects that seem to multiply when counted, or vanish when unobserved. There is a spice so rare and so perfectly preserved that it is said to have come from this lost age; those who smell it report vivid visions of rituals they did not witness. A particular alchemist who sells 'salts for preservation' is actually a priest of the old faith, identifying potential sacrifices among the crowd. The slaves sold here include some who are not entirely human—those with too-knowing eyes who seem to understand languages before they are spoken.

The Saffron Quarter.description: The wealthiest section, where spices from the mysterious East are arrayed in impossible quantity. The air is so saturated with fragrance that breathing becomes intoxicating. Merchants here wear fine robes and calculate prices with mathematical precision.

The Silken Quarter.description: Where bolts of fabric in every color imaginable are displayed and traded. Merchants' fingers are stained with dyes, and the sight of colored cloth billowing in the wind is hypnotic. The silks themselves seem to hold light.

The Central Exchange.description: The true heart of the market, where brokers manage wholesale transactions. Small pavilions serve as offices; ledgers are consulted; agreements are struck that will ripple across continents. The crowd here is dangerous—every face is a calculator.

The Jewel Stalls.description: Where gemstones and worked metal catch the light and throw it back as fractured rainbows. The stones here are of impossible quality, some displaying colors that do not quite exist in nature. Valuations are carried out behind screens of silk.

The Alchemist's Row.description: A narrow passage where merchants of dubious legitimacy sell concoctions, powders, and preserved things in sealed vessels. The air smells of old books, burning herbs, and something else—something ancient.

The Slave Ring.description: The section of the market where human cargo is openly displayed and negotiated. Families are separated, prices are shouted, and the Tharsi merchants conduct business with absolute indifference to the human cost.

The Dolphin Banking House.name: The Dolphin Banking House
The Dolphin Banking House.basicInfo: An imposing fortress of pale marble and granite, its facade adorned with merchant-prince iconography and intricate carvings of trade-routes and sailing vessels. Within, vast chambers of polished stone and wood house ledgers that record the financial secrets of a thousand fortunes. The main hall is a marvel of architecture—a soaring rotunda where shafts of light from high windows illuminate the work of countless accountants and scribes. The walls are lined with safes built into the very stone, and the air is cool, dry, and utterly silent except for the scratch of quills on parchment.
The Dolphin Banking House.hiddenInfo: The Dolphin Banking House is not merely a repository of wealth—it is a front for the merchant-princes' most secret enterprise, the funding of assassination contracts and political destabilization across the known world. The deepest vaults contain not gold, but sealed documents that, if exposed, would topple three kingdoms. The senior accountants are trained to kill without hesitation; many have the calculating precision of predators who learned their art long before they learned mathematics. A massive embezzlement scheme has been unfolding for years, with a senior director siphoning fortunes to fund a rival merchant faction—the other directors suspect but cannot prove it, and fear the retribution that would follow exposure.

The Temple of Mercurius.name: The Temple of Mercurius
The Temple of Mercurius.basicInfo: A sprawling sanctuary dedicated to the god of commerce and cunning, its gardens terraced with fountains and exotic plants. The temple's courtyards host merchants seeking blessings for profitable ventures, and the priests—themselves wealthy traders—mediate disputes and sanctify contracts. The architecture is deliberately ostentatious: columns of polished stone spiral upward, roofs are tiled with copper that gleams like amber, and the inner sanctum is hung with offerings of gold coin and silver jewelry. Pilgrims and merchants mingle on the temple steps, seeking either divine favor or a priest willing to overlook moral considerations.
The Temple of Mercurius.hiddenInfo: The priests of Mercurius are not merely merchants with religious titles—they are the true architects of the merchant-princes' power, wielding influence through the confessionals and private counsel. In the temple's vaults beneath the sanctum, a library exists that should not: records of every deal, every betrayal, every scandal among Tharsis's elite. These records are leverage, and the high priests use them to command absolute obedience from their flock. Whispers suggest that the temple's inner circle has begun performing rituals that predate even the blood-priests, invoking the ancient god of commerce before it was given a Tharsi name—and the results have been disturbingly efficacious.

The Serpent's Wharf.name: The Serpent's Wharf
The Serpent's Wharf.basicInfo: A notorious smugglers' harbor hidden within a rocky cove, accessible only by a narrow channel that requires intimate knowledge of the tides. The wharves here are ramshackle but functional—wooden platforms lashed together with rope, lit by guttering oil lamps. Vessels with unmarked hulls dock here in darkness, their crews moving with practiced efficiency to off-load cargo that will never appear in any manifest. The air reeks of pitch, brine, and decay. This is where forbidden things change hands: banned books, weapons destined for rebellious provinces, slaves destined for worse fates than public auction.
The Serpent's Wharf.hiddenInfo: The Serpent's Wharf is controlled by a woman known only as the Keeper, who once served as a merchant-prince's concubine and is now the only person who could destroy three major houses with whispered secrets. She maintains absolute neutrality—her wharf serves anyone willing to pay, regardless of allegiance—which has made her indispensable to every faction in Tharsis. Her personal quarters sit above the wharf in a tower of dark stone, and rumor claims she has enslaved a furycrafter to bind a protective ward over the harbor. Some sailors claim to have seen ships vanish into the cove and emerge miles away, in waters that should be days' sailing distant.

The House of Exquisite Pleasures.name: The House of Exquisite Pleasures
The House of Exquisite Pleasures.basicInfo: An opulent pleasure-district constructed on multiple levels of terraced stone, where every sensory indulgence is available to those with coin. Pavilions of silk and carved wood house wine-bars, perfume-houses, and intimate chambers. Enslaved musicians play intricate compositions from sunset until dawn. The air is heavy with perfume, wine, and incense. Merchants and trade-lords come here to unwind, accompanied by courtesans trained in arts both refined and scandalous. The streets are lit by colored lanterns, and the sound is one of constant, controlled debauchery.
The House of Exquisite Pleasures.hiddenInfo: The House of Exquisite Pleasures is not merely a pleasure-district—it is a surveillance network of unparalleled sophistication. Every chamber is fitted with hidden passages and observation points; every courtesan is trained to extract secrets from their clients through intimacy and intoxication. The proprietor, Madame Saffron, is one of the merchant-princes' most valuable operatives, with more genuine power than many of them. She knows secrets that could shatter marriages, destroy reputations, and topple merchants from their thrones. Some patrons disappear after their visits, their bodies sometimes found in the harbor. The courtesans are not all enslaved—some are operatives so skilled that even their closest friends do not know their true allegiance.

The Iron Shipyard.name: The Iron Shipyard
The Iron Shipyard.basicInfo: A sprawling complex of docks and construction bays where vessels are built and repaired with Tharsi precision and ruthlessness. The air is thick with smoke from forges, the sound of hammers is constant, and the smell of hot metal mingles with fresh-cut wood. Hundreds of enslaved craftspeople labor under the eyes of overseers, their movements choreographed by necessity and brutality. The merchant-princes' finest vessels emerge from these yards—sleek, fast, and fitted with innovations that keep Tharsis's mercantile dominion unchallenged on the seas.
The Iron Shipyard.hiddenInfo: The Iron Shipyard is where a secret arms-manufacturing enterprise operates in plain sight. Ostensibly building merchant vessels, the master shipwrights are actually constructing warships fitted with experimental furycraft-powered engines, designed in collaboration with rogue elements from the blood-priest faction. The merchant-princes are preparing for a war they believe is coming, but their true enemy may be closer than they think. Certain ships built here are designed to sink—deliberately crafted with hidden flaws that will activate under specific conditions, ensuring that vessels carrying rivals' cargo end up on the ocean floor.

The Silk District.name: The Silk District
The Silk District.basicInfo: A quarter of narrow, winding streets lined with workshops where the finest silk-weavers in the known world practice their craft. Looms of impossible complexity fill every workshop, tended by enslaved artisans whose fingers move with the precision of machines. The streets are draped with bolts of fabric in progress—reds, purples, golds, and colors for which no names exist. The air carries the faint smell of dyes and the sounds of weaving. Here, the most exclusive fabrics for the merchant-princes' courts are created, each bolt worth small fortunes.
The Silk District.hiddenInfo: The Silk District conceals a breeding program conducted by a rogue furycrafter and the merchant-princes' faction. The enslaved weavers are not merely craftspeople—they are selected for specific genetic traits, and certain families have been bred over generations to produce offspring with enhanced sensory perception and manual dexterity. Some of the finest weavers can read intent from the quality of thread, and it is whispered that their creations subtly influence those who wear them. The rarest silks are woven with thread that has been treated with substances derived from blood-priest rituals, granting them properties that should be impossible.

The Grand Auction House.name: The Grand Auction House
The Grand Auction House.basicInfo: An immense amphitheater of white stone with tiered seating rising toward a domed ceiling of polished lapis lazuli inlaid with gold. The central platform is raised high, illuminated by carefully positioned braziers. This is where the most prestigious goods and the most valued human chattels are sold to the highest bidders. The air crackles with anticipation before each auction; the crowd here represents wealth and power incarnate—merchant princes, foreign dignitaries, and collectors of the rare and forbidden.
The Grand Auction House.hiddenInfo: The Grand Auction House is not merely a place of commerce—it is a stage where the merchant-princes' political power is constantly affirmed and challenged. Certain items auctioned here are specifically chosen to send messages: selling a former blood-priest's relic to a merchant-prince is a statement of dominance; withholding a promised lot is an insult that can spark feuds. The auction-masters are expert in reading the room's power dynamics and can manipulate prices and outcomes through subtle theatrical choices. Rumors speak of a hidden vault beneath the amphitheater where items too dangerous or forbidden even for the private auctions are kept—and that sometimes, on certain moonless nights, these items are offered to bidders from beyond the conventional elite.

Kalthorn War-Moot.name: Kalthorn War-Moot
Kalthorn War-Moot.basicInfo: The greatest gathering-place of the Vallari tribes, a sprawling encampment spread across a sheltered valley where a dozen war-camps circle a central stone-ringed moat-ground. Smoke from a hundred watchfires rises toward the granite peaks. Here war-chiefs meet to settle blood-feuds, divide hunting territories, and plan raids against the limes. The air thrums with drum-song and the calls of shamans reading omens in the wind. Massive standing-stones mark the boundaries of each tribe's hearth-lands, and the ground itself is scarred with the footsteps of centuries.
Kalthorn War-Moot.hiddenInfo: Beneath the central moot-ground lies a burial barrow of an elder from the Age of Heroes—the shamans alone know of the skeletal hand that sometimes grasps at the roots of the standing-stones when the veil grows thin. A secret rivalry simmers between the Vallari and the Morthane clans who attend; three summers past, a blood-debt was sworn but never settled, and the Kalthorn moot walks a knife-edge between alliance and slaughter.

The Hearth Circle.description: The central gathering ground ringed by weathered monoliths and fire-pits, where war-chiefs speak beneath the open sky. Smoke coils through endless argument and treaty-making.

The Vallari Encampment.description: Dozens of leather-and-fur tents clustered in semi-circles, their standards bearing fang and talon symbols. War-bands sharpen weapons and sing songs of old glories and coming blood-feuds.

The Morthane Lands.description: A separate cluster of war-tents flying the Morthane colors—grey wolf and storm-cloud. The air here crackles with barely-restrained violence, their shamans often at odds with their Vallari cousins.

The Shamans' Grove.description: A stand of ancient gnarled oaks where ritual-fires burn through the night. Bone totems hang from branches, and the shamans perform auguries by studying the flight of ravens and the flow of the wind.

The Feast Grounds.description: An enormous natural amphitheater where roasted game is split among the clans and mead flows freely. Warriors boast of deeds while bards weave new epics into being.

Hardhelm Hold.name: Hardhelm Hold
Hardhelm Hold.basicInfo: The fortress-stronghold of Jarl Brennvik the Scarred, carved into the slope of a granite tor. High timber palisades crowned with sharpened stakes ring the compound, and the main hall is a cavernous longhouse with a smoke-hole the size of a man's torso. War-trophies hang from the rafters—shields of fallen enemies, their paint still vivid. The Jarl rules through strength and cunning, and his hall has never been breached by outside arms. The valley below holds the winter-camp of three hundred warriors and their families.
Hardhelm Hold.hiddenInfo: Brennvik's younger son, deemed too weak in body but sharp in wit, has begun communing with a shade-spirit that dwells in the deeper caverns beneath the hold. The Jarl suspects sorcery but dare not act without proof—and the son has already begun subtly influencing his elder brother's decisions, planting seeds of doubt that may one day tear the succession apart. The deepest crypts hold an artifact of the Old Stone Folk, half-buried and forgotten.

The High Longhouse.description: The great feasting and judgment hall where Brennvik holds court. The hearth-fire burns perpetually, and the walls echo with the voices of ancestors through carved runestones.

The Palisade Yards.description: The open courtyard within the timber walls where warriors drill and smiths work at their forges. The clang of hammer on metal is constant.

The Warriors' Barracks.description: Long low buildings where the war-bands sleep in shifts. Weapons racks line every wall, and the smell of oil and leather mingles with the acrid smoke of the cookfires.

The Gate Towers.description: Twin timber towers flanking the massive double-gates of the hold. Watchmen keep eternal vigil, and signal-horns hang ready for alarm or triumph.

The Shamans' Hut.description: A small, fur-hung dwelling attached to the longhouse where the hold's shamans read bones and prepare herb-potions. Smoke of sacred plants winds through the air.

Thornwood Hunting Camp.name: Thornwood Hunting Camp
Thornwood Hunting Camp.basicInfo: A seasonal settlement of three dozen hunters and their families nestled in the heart of a vast pine forest, where ancient trees tower overhead and the forest floor is thick with moss and fallen needles. Drying-racks laden with strips of venison and elk-meat stand beside tents of hide and bone. The hunters are relentless trackers, following game trails that cut through the wilds for leagues. A sacred pool nearby is said to draw the great beasts for their drink at dawn.
Thornwood Hunting Camp.hiddenInfo: The hunters have discovered a wounded creature in the deep woods—neither wholly animal nor wholly human, bearing markings that the shamans claim mark it as touched by the Old Magic. Some wish to slay it for its pelt; others whisper that it is a test sent by the spirits. A hunter named Kess has begun to secretly feed it, believing herself chosen.

Stormrider's Crag.name: Stormrider's Crag
Stormrider's Crag.basicInfo: A high windswept plateau crowned by the ruins of an ancient stone circle, perched so high that clouds gather at its feet. Here the air itself seems alive, cracking with strange energies that the shamans claim are the breath of the storm-spirits. A handful of stone shelters offer refuge from the gales, and ritual-dancers come here in autumn to enact the Rite of Calling, summoning the winter's first snow with drum and chant.
Stormrider's Crag.hiddenInfo: The ruins are far older than the tribes remember—carvings in an unknown tongue spiral up the standing-stones, and one of them hums audibly when the wind blows from the north, as if it were singing to something beyond the veil of the world. Explorers who linger past dark report seeing lights between the stones, dancing in patterns that follow no natural law.

Deepmire Shaman's Grove.name: Deepmire Shaman's Grove
Deepmire Shaman's Grove.basicInfo: A sacred sanctuary hidden within a vast bog where ancient twisted willows rise from black water, their branches hung with bone charms and feathered totems. The shamans of a dozen tribes gather here in the deepest winter to share knowledge and perform the Rite of Echoes, a ceremony that allows them to speak with the spirits of the dead. The air is thick with incense-smoke and the croaking of unseen birds. Raised wooden paths—some ancient, some newly laid—guide travelers across the treacherous waters.
Deepmire Shaman's Grove.hiddenInfo: Beneath the bog's surface lies the sunken temple of a civilization that predates the tribes by uncounted ages. Shamans sometimes report receiving visions of its chambers in their trance-dreams. The bog itself is sentient in some fashion—it swallows the unwary and sometimes releases them days later, changed, speaking in voices not their own. A great spirit-beast dwells in the deepest mire, glimpsed only as a vast shape moving through the black water.

The Inner Circle.description: The heart of the grove where the primary ritual-fires burn and ancient oak-thrones face each other in a semi-circle. Here shamans debate and share visions through the night.

The Bone Walk.description: A narrow wooden boardwalk raised above the bog-water, lined with hanging totems and charms that mark the path of shamans between gathering seasons.

The Spirit Pool.description: A perfectly circular area of still dark water where shamans see visions of the dead. The surface sometimes ripples without any wind or touch.

The Willow Sanctuary.description: A grove of gnarled willows where shamans meditate in solitude, their tents nestled among the ancient roots and twisted branches.

Frosthelm's Pass.name: Frosthelm's Pass
Frosthelm's Pass.basicInfo: A narrow mountain pass where a frozen river cuts between granite cliffs, its surface thick with blue ice that never fully thaws even in the height of summer. War-bands and trading parties cross here, and a handful of stone shelters mark the way-stations. The roar of the river beneath the ice is constant and eerie, and the pass is treacherous—ice-bridges collapse without warning, and the cold is murderous to the unprepared.
Frosthelm's Pass.hiddenInfo: Three winters past, an entire war-band vanished crossing the pass. The shamans say the river-spirit claimed them for some ancient debt unpaid. Hunters sometimes report finding fragments of armor or weapons embedded in the ice, emerging from deep layers as if the ice itself were slowly vomiting up the dead. A great ice-troll is said to dwell in the caverns above the pass, though sightings are rare.

Darkmarch Forest.name: Darkmarch Forest
Darkmarch Forest.basicInfo: A vast, primordial forest of towering pine and ancient oak where the canopy is so thick that twilight reigns eternal even at midday. The forest is dark, brooding, and wild—herds of elk and dire-wolves roam its depths, and the tribes speak of it with a mixture of hunger and dread. Great standing-stones mark the tribal hunting-territories within, and hunters sometimes disappear into the gloom and never return. The soil is rich with the loam of ten thousand years.
Darkmarch Forest.hiddenInfo: The forest is home to a civilization of Wild Folk—neither human nor wholly beast, who existed before the tribes came north. They are said to guard treasures of the Old Age: weapons that never break, cloaks that grant invisibility, and a horn that summons aid from the spirits. The trees themselves are said to communicate through the vibration of their roots, and the forest slowly grows back roads and paths to isolate intruders. A hunter who enters the forest's heart alone for three days gains the gift of speaking with beasts—or the curse of becoming one.

Wolfstone Standing-Stones.name: Wolfstone Standing-Stones
Wolfstone Standing-Stones.basicInfo: An ancient circle of weathered granite monoliths arranged on an open hilltop, each stone carved with the likenesses of wolves and warriors locked in eternal struggle. The site has no permanent settlement, but war-bands often pause here to perform blood-oaths and challenge-duels beneath the open sky. The stones hum audibly when thunder rolls, and tales speak of them as the petrified remains of warriors from the Age of Wrath.
Wolfstone Standing-Stones.hiddenInfo: One standing-stone bears a crack that runs its length—an old scar, not a flaw of the earth. Within that crack, shamans claim, dwells a bound spirit of immense power, sworn to aid any who speak the true name carved in runes that only three shamans alive can read. A war-band that attempted to claim those stones during the last great moon-dark was found turned to stone themselves, frozen mid-motion in attitudes of terror and awe.

Whitewraith Waterfall.name: Whitewraith Waterfall
Whitewraith Waterfall.basicInfo: A spectacular cataract that thunder down from a cliff-face into a deep pool, its mist perpetually swirling into ghostly shapes. The roar is deafening, and the spray rises so high that rainbows dance in its vapor even on the darkest days. The Morthane tribe claims this as sacred ground—here, they say, the boundary between the mortal realm and the spirit-realm grows thin. Shamans come in the season of green growth to stand in the mist and receive visions of the year to come.
Whitewraith Waterfall.hiddenInfo: The mist conceals a hidden cave behind the waterfall, accessible only when the water-flow lessens in the depths of winter. Within lies evidence of habitation by something—or someone—far older than any tribe: carved pipes that produce haunting tones, and bones arranged in patterns that suggest ritual significance. Those who spend a night in the cave return changed, claiming to have conversed with the voice of the waters themselves.

The Iron Raiders' Winter-Hold.name: The Iron Raiders' Winter-Hold
The Iron Raiders' Winter-Hold.basicInfo: A formidable stockaded settlement of the most feared war-band in the Wilds, the Iron Raiders, who number nearly two hundred strong. Their hold sits in a defensible valley where swift horses can be raised and supplies stockpiled. The buildings are sturdy timber and stone, and the reputation of the warband—for ferocity in raid and cunning in ambush—draws glory-seekers and desperate survivors who wish to join their ranks. The Raider-Queen, Skaln the Scarred, rules with absolute authority, and her word is final law.
The Iron Raiders' Winter-Hold.hiddenInfo: Skaln is not what she appears. She is bound to a spirit of ancient war by a pact made in her youth, and her inhuman strength and cunning are gifts of that bond. The pact comes due in three winters' time, and Skaln has begun secretly preparing a successor—one of her lieutenants who does not yet know she will press the same binding upon him. A rival faction within the Raiders, led by her own brother, has begun to suspect the truth and is quietly gathering followers.

The Raider's Hall.description: The great longhouse where Skaln holds court and the war-band gathers for councils. Weapons and trophies line the walls, and the air smells of smoke, leather, and old glory.

The Armory Yards.description: An open compound where raiders drill, sharpen weapons, and practice their deadly arts. The clash and ring of steel is constant.

The Stables.description: A long timber structure housing dozens of fierce horses bred for speed and strength, used for the swift raids that make the Iron Raiders legendary.

The Barracks.description: Long stone buildings where raiders sleep between campaigns, their sleeping-furs marked with their personal insignia.

The Smith's Workshop.description: A forge that rings constantly with the hammer-blows of master smiths, crafting weapons and armor that are sought throughout the northern wilds.

Vespaetera.name: Vespaetera
Vespaetera.basicInfo: An ancient twilight-city sprawling beneath a dim amber sun that never quite brightens. Its marble columns—worn smooth by centuries of wind—rise from twilit streets where scholars in flowing robes argue philosophy in whispers. The air carries incense and something older: the ghost-scent of a hundred forgotten festivals. Buildings of pale stone cascade down a shallow hill toward a central plaza where a sphinx-statue—weathered blind—watches over everything. Traders of esoteric knowledge gather here; those seeking lost texts and stranger seekers alike are drawn to its perpetual dusk.
Vespaetera.hiddenInfo: Vespaetera's founding texts—inscribed on hidden temple walls beneath the plaza—describe the city as a refuge built by those who fled a cataclysm 'when bright suns burned too fierce.' The scholars guard a terrible secret: their city is slowly sinking, subsiding into the earth by mere inches each year. The priesthood interprets this not as doom but as the world's patient healing. Beneath the plaza lies a sealed chamber containing a single artifact—a lens of polished obsidian that, when held to moonlight, reveals written words in a language predating human speech.

The Marble Plaza.description: The heart of Vespaetera, a vast expanse of pale stone where the blind sphinx-statue presides. Ancient mosaic patterns hint at star-maps and mathematical equations worn nearly invisible by footsteps.

The Library of Dusk.description: A labyrinthine structure of twelve connected towers holding scrolls in languages living and dead. The air here is peculiarly still; readers whisper their findings to one another in reverent tones. Some books are shelved spine-inward, their titles deliberately hidden.

The Philosopher's Quarter.description: Narrow streets lined with debate-halls where scholars gather around low tables. The sound of voices—sometimes heated, often cryptic—drifts from half-shuttered windows. The architecture is deliberately asymmetrical, as though the buildings themselves argue with one another.

The Temple Precinct.description: Sacred spaces dedicated to gods whose names appear in no common pantheon. Offerings of wine, ash, and written prayers hang from branches of ancient olive trees. The temples themselves are small and shadowed, their priests solitary and cryptic.

The Scholar's Garden.description: An unexpected haven of cultivation—strange plants with silvery leaves grow under the dim sun, tended by silent gardeners. Pathways wind between herb beds and curious stone sculptures. The garden feels timeless, as though seasons barely touch it.

Sarcophagi of Antiquus.name: Sarcophagi of Antiquus
Sarcophagi of Antiquus.basicInfo: A vast necropolis spread across a plateau of cracked stone, its sprawling ruins older than memory itself. Thousands of carved sarcophagi rise like a marble forest, each engraved with names in scripts that shift in the twilight—sometimes legible, sometimes rendered in shadow-tongues. The ground beneath is treacherous with debris: fragments of funerary masks, shattered urns, offerings corroded past recognition. Strange pillars bearing no inscriptions stand among the tombs, their purpose long forgotten. Relic-hunters and grave-scholars thread the aisles in solemn procession; the place hums with a quiet that feels almost alive.
Sarcophagi of Antiquus.hiddenInfo: The sarcophagi are not truly sealed—their lids have been opened countless times over epochs, yet the dead remain. Grave-scholars have discovered that the burial vaults do not contain bodies at all, but rather ash arranged into intricate geometric patterns. The pillars are older than the tombs themselves and are carved from a stone that does not match the local geology; they appear to have been transported from somewhere else—somewhere impossibly distant. At the necropolis' heart lies the Sealed Vault, a structure buried three stories below ground whose entrance was deliberately collapsed by ancient hands. Local legends speak of a custodian-spirit who walks the aisles at dusk, ensuring the dead remain undisturbed; a few travelers claim to have seen a figure of pale stone moving between the tombs.

The Outer Aisles.description: The perimeter of the necropolis, where most accessible tombs cluster. Names are still legible here, and offerings left by visiting pilgrims decorate the sarcophagi. The air is thick with incense-ash.

The Central Gallery.description: A processional way running through the heart of the necropolis, flanked by the oldest and grandest sarcophagi. The pillars of unknown stone rise here, casting shadows that seem deeper than they should be.

The Temple of the Custodian.description: A small, roofless structure of pale marble where pilgrim-scholars leave offerings and speak prayers to the guardian-spirit. The floor is marked with star-paths and ritual circles.

The Sealed Vault's Approach.description: The entrance to the buried chamber, deliberately collapsed long ago. Stone blocks are stacked with deliberate care, and warning-glyphs are carved into every surface. The ground here feels unstable, as though something lies restless beneath.

The Shattered Quarter.description: The oldest part of the necropolis, where tombs have begun to crumble into ruin. Names are illegible here; some sarcophagi bear no engravings at all. This section feels distinctly haunted.

The Star-Temple of Xithelis.name: The Star-Temple of Xithelis
The Star-Temple of Xithelis.basicInfo: Perched upon a solitary stone peak overlooking the twilight plains, this temple is dedicated to a god of ancient and alien aspect. Its architecture defies easy description: curved walls of deep blue stone covered in celestial carvings—constellations that do not match any known sky. At its center, a great dome of dark crystal lets in the starlight while filtering out the dim sun. Pilgrims climb the endless spiral stairs to reach it, driven by visions and whispers they cannot quite articulate. The priests here are few, aged, and speak in riddles. The temple hums with a low frequency that some say is the god itself breathing.
The Star-Temple of Xithelis.hiddenInfo: Xithelis is not a god of this age—the temple's oldest records identify it as a power from the void-before-stars, something that descended when the world was young and dark. The great dome was not built but found, already ancient when the first priests arrived. Pilgrims who meditate beneath it often see visions of impossible geometries and hear singing in tongues. The priesthood has discovered that the temple's location is precisely aligned with a star that appears only once every thousand years—and that star is due to appear in mere months. The final hidden chamber beneath the temple contains a star-map carved into the floor, showing not the sky above but something far, far deeper: the architecture of the void itself.

The Outer Sanctum.description: The temple's entrance and lower levels, where pilgrims remove their sandals and leave offerings of starlight-caught-in-bottles and written prayers on stone tablets. The walls are warm to the touch.

The Spiral Ascension.description: An impossibly long staircase spiraling upward within the tower, its steps worn smooth by millennia of pilgrims' feet. Windows open onto the twilight plains far below. The air grows colder as one ascends.

The Crystalline Dome.description: The temple's heart—a vast circular chamber beneath the great dome of dark crystal. Starlight filters through in patterns that seem to move with purpose. The floor is marked with constellation-circles where pilgrims kneel in meditation.

The Archive of Visions.description: A chamber whose walls are inscribed with thousands of pilgrims' testimonies—descriptions of visions seen beneath the dome, written in different hands, different ages, some barely legible. No two accounts agree, yet patterns echo through them.

The Priest's Quarters.description: A sparse, austere collection of cells where the temple's priesthood dwells in silent contemplation. Their few possessions suggest lives dedicated entirely to understanding Xithelis. A single locked door leads deeper still.

Twilight Market of Sudara.name: Twilight Market of Sudara
Twilight Market of Sudara.basicInfo: A sprawling bazaar nestled in a ravine where perpetual mist pools like water, cloaking the marketplace in a permanent haze. Merchants have erected canopies of silk and ancient cloth; lamps burn with blue flames that cast everything in ghostly shadow. The air smells of exotic spices, leather, and something older—myrrh and dust from lands long swallowed by sand. Vendors call their wares in a dozen languages: lost manuscripts, reliquaries containing nameless saints' bones, preserved herbs that bloom only under starlight, instruments carved from metals that do not tarnish. Scholars and relic-hunters move between stalls like ghosts themselves, haggling in hushed tones over prices that seem to shift with the mist.
Twilight Market of Sudara.hiddenInfo: The marketplace operates under an ancient truce—all violence is forbidden within its bounds, enforced by merchant-knights whose identities are concealed. A council of elder merchants known as the Keepers of the Mist actually govern the market; they meet in a hidden chamber beneath the main bazaar to decide disputes and oversee the mysterious caravans that arrive only under starlight. The market's true treasure lies in the merchants themselves: many are not human at all, but something older, something that dwells in perpetual twilight by nature. The most coveted stalls sell items whose origins are impossible—tools from dead civilizations, maps of lands that no longer exist, books whose pages are written on materials that should not hold ink.

The Scholar-King's Twilight Court.name: The Scholar-King's Twilight Court
The Scholar-King's Twilight Court.basicInfo: An isolated palace carved from pale stone and set within a walled garden of impossible geometry. The architecture is severe and elegant: columns of marble, courtyards opening onto courtyards, a library wing that stretches for what seems like miles. The Scholar-King rules in absentia—said to be perpetually engaged in philosophical debate with his own reflection in a pool of still water—while administrators run the day-to-day affairs of his small realm. Court scholars conduct research into the nature of consciousness, time, and the thin places between worlds. Visitors are few and carefully vetted; to be granted audience is considered a great honor and a peculiar curse.
The Scholar-King's Twilight Court.hiddenInfo: The Scholar-King is not a mortal ruler but something far older—a consciousness that has inhabited the court for three thousand years, assuming a new physical form each century when the old one grows too worn for further use. The pool where he conducts his endless debate is not water but something else entirely; courtiers swear that if you gaze into it, you see not your reflection but the reflection of a place you have never visited but somehow recognize. The court's greatest research project, hidden even from most of the scholars themselves, concerns the nature of the twilight itself: Is it a natural phenomenon, or was the dim sun deliberately dimmed? And if so, by whom, and for what purpose?

The Reception Hall.description: Where visitors are greeted and assessed by the court's chamberlain. Tapestries depicting ancient kings line the walls, their faces rendered deliberately indistinct.

The Garden Courtyards.description: A series of connected gardens of impossible geometry—paths that seem to lead in contradictory directions, fountains that flow upward, plants that bloom only at specific hours.

The Library Wing.description: An expansive collection of treatises on philosophy, mathematics, and esoteric knowledge. Shelves stretch upward into shadow; some sections are cordoned off and marked with warning-seals.

The Scholar's Quarters.description: Where resident scholars conduct their research. Small, austere cells cluster around larger chambers where collaborative work takes place. The atmosphere is intense and hushed.

The Pool of Reflection.description: The Scholar-King's chamber, where he sits perpetually in meditation beside a pool of liquid that might be water or might be something else entirely. Few are permitted entry; fewer still emerge unchanged.

The Forgotten Library of Thassial.name: The Forgotten Library of Thassial
